Once there was a puppy, who was so scared of everything. He was always so scared of new sights and sounds. One day, while he was exploring the backyard, he saw something he'd never seen before. It scared him so he ran away. As he was running away, he felt something bite him in the leg. He was so scared he ran back inside the house. He didn't want to go back outside again. The next day, he went out to explore the yard again, but this time he was more careful. He kept an eye out for scary sights and he looked around for anything that might bite him. He was much braver than before and eventually he didn't mind exploring the backyard anymore. He soon remembered that not all sights are scary and that sometimes things that bite can be friendly too. This week's post is by Laura Irvine, who is just about to start her second year reading Mathematics at Murray Edwards College, Cambridge. Thanks very much, Laura! First of all, what is the Cantor set? To form the Cantor set, start with the closed interval (this means and are included in the interval) and remove the middle open third of the interval (i.e. remove (, ) where the curved brackets mean the interval is open, so and are not themselves in the interval). You should be left with two disjoint closed intervals: and . I'm going to call this step the first iteration. Then do the same thing to each of these intervals: remove the middle third of each to get the new intervals as , , , . Then remove the middle third of each of these intervals. Keep repeating this process and the Cantor set is the set of all the points in the interval that are never removed. So the first few steps of the process look like this: Do you understand how this set is formed? Can you think of some points that are in the Cantor set? Well, will never be removed: the first closed interval after the iteration would be so will be in the infinite intersection of the first interval of each step. The other interval endpoints are points in the set too. It turns out that there are also points that are in the set that aren't interval endpoints. An interesting and, in my opinion, rather surprising property of the Cantor set is that it has measure , despite being an uncountable set! The fact it is uncountable means there is no way of writing all the numbers in the Cantor set in a list. So, intuitively, this is saying that if all the points in the Cantor set were lined up next to each other, the line would have length and yet there are infinitely many points in the set. How weird is that?! How can we show the Cantor set is uncountable? Well, it would be good if we could show that there's a surjection from the set to another set that we already know is uncountable. This would show that there are at least as many points in the Cantor set as there are in this other uncountable set. We know that there are uncountably many real numbers so how about trying to construct a surjection from the Cantor set to the real numbers? First, we need to work in a ternary system. This is a similar idea to the binary system (which is where numbers are written using just the digits and ) or the decimal system (where you use the digits to ). However, in a ternary system there are three digits that can be used: , and . To write a number in the decimal system, you might imagine column headings of units, tens, hundreds etc. (i.e. , , , , ). In binary, you would imagine column headings of , , , , , etc. (i.e. , , , , ). In ternary, the column headings would be (from right to left) , , , , . So, to write a given number, say , in ternary, think what the biggest power of is that is still less than, in this case, . So that will be . goes into twice so in the ` column', write . so we now see what the largest power of three that is less than is. It's and only goes into once so write in the ` column'. Carry on in this way. so in the ` column' write . so put in the final column. So, in ternary, . Does that make sense? What would be in ternary? For numbers between and , the column headings will be , , , etc. So would be and would be . Just as another example, in ternary. Can you see how I calculated that? In the first iteration when forming the Cantor set, all numbers which don't have or as their beginning in ternary are removed. You might think: but what about ? But that's alright, because in ternary, is the same as . Then, in the second iteration, get rid of all the numbers that don't have or in the second position after the decimal point. And so on, so we can see that in the Cantor set, every number is made up of s and s. Now, what we have to do is to map every in any number in the Cantor set to a . So, for example, would become . This will give the full set of numbers in the interval in binary. This means there is a mapping which has its image as the whole of the interval . That is, there is a surjection from the Cantor set to all the real numbers in the interval . Since the real numbers are uncountable, so the Cantor set must be! Here comes the cool part! Theorem The Cantor set has measure . OK, so how can we prove that? Well, how about calculating how much of the interval is removed when forming the Cantor set. Then we could subtract this number from : Measure of Cantor set = (Measure of the interval ) - (All the stuff removed from to get the Cantor set). So, to form the Cantor set, we started with an interval of measure and subtracted . Then of each of the remaining thirds, we took the middle thirds of each away (so we subtracted ). Then there were intervals, each of which had its middle third removed so was subtracted. Then were removed, then and so on. Can you see what the difference between the measure of the and the iteration would be? Well, the size of each interval being removed is a third smaller than in the previous step so the denominator will be . The numerator, on the other hand, doubles with each step as each interval splits into two new intervals. So it is . Therefore, all these intervals that we're removing add up to . Now is a geometric progression with starting value and where each term is of the previous term so the common ratio is . The formula for the sum to infinity of a geometric progression is so . so the measure of the Cantor set is . So the theorem is proved! Santa Monica is a beach town in California. [more car sounds; this continues, alternating with MOT and Wal laughing] Title: How to De-ice Your Windshield Introduction: Driving with an icy windshield is dangerous and can lead to accidents. It's crucial to de-ice your windshield properly before hitting the road during winter months. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to do this safely and effectively. Materials Needed: 1. Ice scraper or snow brush 2. Cold water (optional) 3. Lukewarm cloth (optional) 4. De-icing spray or solution (commercial or homemade) 5. Rubbing alcohol and water mixture (homemade alternative) 6. A pair of gloves (recommended but optional) Step 1: Prepare Your Tools Gather all necessary materials mentioned above. Having everything ready at hand makes the process more efficient and less frustrating. Using gloves can help keep your hands warm while protecting them from sharp edges of the ice. Step 2: Turn On Defrost Mode Start your car and turn on the defrost mode. The heated air blowing onto the windscreen will aid melting and removing the ice. Please note that if the temperature outside is extremely low, this alone may not be enough to remove heavy frost quickly. Key Tip: Patience is essential when using the defrost setting as it takes time to work effectively. Never pour hot water directly onto the cold glass surface as sudden temperature changes could cause damage such as cracking. Step 3: Use Commercial De-Icing Spray If available, spray the commercial de-icer evenly over the entire frozen area. These products contain ingredients designed specifically to melt ice fast without damaging the vehicle's paint job. Wait a few minutes for the product to take effect. For those who prefer making their own solution, mix one part rubbing alcohol (isopropyl) with two parts water in a spray bottle and apply it similarly. Alcohol has a lower freezing point than water which allows it to dissolve the bond between the ice and the window faster than water alone would. Step 4: Scrape Off the Softened Ice After waiting for about 2-5 minutes depending on the thickness of the ice, start gently scraping off the softened layer of ice using your ice scraper or snow brush. Begin from the top corners and move towards the center. Try to chip away large chunks instead of small pieces to speed up the process. Key Guideline: Always hold the scraper handle firmly and push forward rather than pulling back. Pulling motions might put strain on your wrist and are generally less effective. Also, avoid applying too much pressure so you don't scratch the glass accidentally. Step 5: Remove Remaining Residue Use the dry side of your snow brush or squeegee to wipe away any remaining residue after most of the ice has been removed. Make sure there aren't any spots left uncleared especially near the wipers where visibility is critical. Additional Step (Optional): For particularly stubborn areas, dampen a lukewarm cloth slightly and place it over the spot. Let it sit for a couple of minutes then try scrubbing again with the scraper. Remember never to use boiling or very hot water because extreme temperature shifts can shock and potentially harm the glass. Conclusion: With these steps followed correctly, de-icing your windshield should no longer pose difficulties. Regular maintenance like parking under cover whenever possible and covering the windscreen overnight during harsh weather conditions can also reduce buildup significantly. Stay safe on the roads! Title: How to Add a Family Member on Spotify Using an Android Device Spotify is a popular music streaming service that offers a family plan, which allows up to six people to enjoy premium features under one account at a reduced cost. This guide will walk you through adding a family member to your Spotify account using an Android device. **Step 1: Launch the Spotify app on your Android device.** Start by finding the Spotify app on your home screen or app drawer and tapping on its icon. The Spotify logo consists of a green circle with three curved black lines inside it, also known as 'spot-drops'. Launching the app brings you to the main interface, where you can access various options like searching for songs, browsing playlists, and managing your account settings. **Step 2: Log into your Premium Family account.** Ensure you are logged into the Spotify Premium Family account, as only the administrator (or 'manager') of this type of account has permission to add new members. If you're already logged in, proceed to the next step; otherwise, tap on the 'Profile' tab located at the bottom right corner of the screen and select 'Log in...' from the menu. Enter your credentials, then hit 'LOG IN' to continue. **Step 3: Access Account Settings.** Tap on the 'Settings' gear icon found in the upper-right corner of the Home screen or Profile tab. Here, you will find several options related to your profile, notification preferences, and other functionalities. Scrolling down, look for 'Account', then press it to view more information regarding your subscription details and payment methods. **Step 4: Go to the 'Family' section.** Under the 'Account Overview' page, locate the option labeled 'Manage Your Family Plan.' Tap on it to be redirected to the family management portal within the app. Once there, you will see all existing members associated with your family plan along with their profiles and avatars. At the top right corner, you should notice a plus sign (+) - pressing this button opens a dialog box prompting you to invite someone to join your family plan. **Key Tips:** * Only users who have subscribed to Spotify Premium Family plans can add additional family members. * Make sure the person being invited has a separate individual Spotify account before sending out invitations. **Guidelines:** * Invite individuals responsibly, ensuring they meet the eligibility requirements set forth by Spotify, such as residing at the same address. * Refrain from sharing login credentials publicly since doing so may violate Spotify's terms of service and lead to suspension or termination of accounts. **Step 5: Send an invitation via email.** After clicking the plus sign (+), enter the email address associated with the recipient's Spotify account. Then click 'Send Invitation' to dispatch the request. An automated message containing further steps will be sent to the provided email address. **Step 6: Wait for acceptance confirmation.** Once the invitation has been delivered, wait patiently for the recipient to accept it. Upon accepting, they will receive full access to the benefits offered by your Spotify Premium Family plan. Meanwhile, keep track of pending invitations through the family management portal until everyone accepts them. By following these simple yet thorough instructions, you can successfully add a family member to your Spotify Premium Family account using an Android device. Enjoy sharing your favorite jams together while taking advantage of exclusive perks tailored specifically for families! Title: How to Create Negative Space Nail Art Negative space nail art is a popular and modern trend in nail design that involves leaving parts of the natural nail unpainted, creating negative spaces which contrast with bold colors or intricate patterns. This type of nail art can be simple yet elegant, making it suitable for both beginners and advanced nail artists. Here's an easy-to-follow guide on achieving stunning negative space nail designs. ### Things you'll need: * Base coat * Clear topcoat * Nail polish in two complementary shades (for this example, we will use white and navy blue) * A thin brush or detailing tool * Nail stickers, tape, or hole punch reinforcement labels (optional) * Cleanup brush and acetone (if needed) **Step 1: Preparation** Start by trimming your nails to your preferred shape and length. File any rough edges using a fine-grit file. Apply base coat to protect your natural nails from staining and ensure better adhesion for the nail polish. Allow the base coat to dry completely before moving onto the next step. **Step 2: Applying the First Color** Choose one color as your primary shade - let's begin with white. Paint all your nails except for the area where you want the negative space to appear. Be sure not to paint over the entire surface of the nail; leave some room for the second color. It might help to rest the edge of a business card against your cuticle while painting to create clean lines. Wait for the first layer to dry before applying another coat if necessary. Key tip: Keep steady hands and take your time during application. Thick layers may cause smudging or bubbling. **Step 3: Masking Off Areas (Optional)** If you find it challenging to freehand precise lines, consider using tools like striping tape, hole punch reinforcements, or even regular Scotch tape to mask off sections of your nail. After applying your base color, place these materials strategically to cover the areas you wish to remain untouched. Then proceed to paint the exposed portions with your second color. Once dried, carefully remove the tape. Guideline: When working with multiple coats, wait at least two minutes between applications to prevent lifting or peeling. **Step 4: Creating Patterns within Negative Spaces** For more complex designs, once the initial base color has fully dried, use a thin brush dipped in the secondary color (navy blue) to draw geometric shapes, curves, or other abstract forms within the negative spaces created earlier. If you make mistakes, don't worry! Acetone and a cleanup brush can fix most errors. Key Tip: Practice drawing your chosen pattern on paper first until you feel confident enough to recreate it on your nails. **Step 5: Adding Final Details** After letting the last layer dry thoroughly, seal your work with a clear topcoat to add shine and prolong wear. Use a generous but controlled amount to avoid pooling around the cuticles. Let the topcoat set completely before doing anything else to avoid damaging your masterpiece. Guideline: To maintain the longevity of your manicure, avoid contact with water, harsh chemicals, and oils immediately after completing your negative space nail art. By following these steps meticulously, you should now have beautiful negative space nail art worthy of showing off. Remember, practice makes perfect - so keep trying different styles and techniques until you discover what works best for you. Happy painting! Let me play outside after I take a nap? Tutorial: How to Cite UpToDate in APA, MLA, and Chicago Style UpToDate is an evidence-based clinical decision support resource that is widely used by healthcare professionals to make informed medical decisions. When using information from UpToDate in academic or research settings, proper citation is essential to avoid plagiarism and give credit to the original authors. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of citing UpToDate in various styles, including APA, MLA, and Chicago. Step 1: Identify the relevant information Before creating a citation, gather all necessary details about the source material from UpToDate. These include: * Title of topic or subtopic (optional) * Author(s)/Editor(s): Full names, initials if available * Edition: If applicable * Publisher: Wolters Kluwer Health * Publication date: Month Day, Year * Page numbers (if printed version) Key tip: Although page numbers are typically included in citations, they may be omitted when referencing electronic sources like UpToDate. However, do include them if you have access to a printout with pagination. Step 2: Format the citation based on your required style The following sections provide examples and guidelines for formatting UpToDate citations according to APA, MLA, and Chicago styles. APA Style (7th edition) For online resources without page numbers, include paragraph numbers if possible. Use the abbreviation "para." followed by the number. General format: Author(s). (Year, Month Date). Topic title [Subtopic title]. In UpToDate, Resource Type. Retrieved from https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search Example: Smith, J., & Johnson, K. (2023, January 5). Diabetes mellitus type 2 [Clinical manifestations]. In UpToDate, Disease Information. Retrieved from <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/diabetes-mellitus-type-2-clinical-manifestations> MLA Style (8th edition) Include author's last name, first name, publication year, month day, and full article title enclosed in quotation marks. Follow this information with the name of the website, publisher, URL, and the accessed date. I don't know that she's never some of them are not readily identifiable. Have the children fill in the blank in the sentence by printing the name of the character. Then, have the students draw a picture of the character on the same piece of paper. The terrestrial mammals are fewer in number of species than lizards, but they are huge in individual numbers, and far more important in the life of the terrestrial biomes. Their ability to move from place to place and adapt has made them a most effective group. Many mammals live in cold places. These mammals have thick hair or blubber to keep them warm. Others may live in rainforests. On land the rodents (rats, mice) are hugely successful, more common in numbers than any other mammals. Large mammals on land have been hunted to extinction in some parts of the world, but the ones which remain are now better protected. Listening to Earthquakes Created by Andy Michael, USGS, and Daniel Ross, 12 year old USGS Volunteer for Science. When we listen to music we hear a wide range of sound frequencies or notes. When we feel an earthquake we feel a wide range of frequencies but unlike music the notes are less distinct. People describing what they felt during an earthquake may call it a "sharp jolt" or a "rolling motion." The sharp jolt is due to high frequencies while the rolling motion is due to low frequencies. What you feel during an earthquake is a product of the earthquake itself, how far you are from the earthquake, and the types of rocks between you and the earthquake. To learn about this we will convert the shaking during earthquakes to sound and then listen to the sounds. Converting Shaking to SoundWhen a loudspeaker produces sound it shakes. During an earthquake the ground shakes. To convert the earthquake shaking to sound we make the loudspeaker shake the same way the ground did. But, if we really did that the sound would be too low for people to hear. So, we speed things up so that the loudspeaker moves back and forth much faster than the ground did and then we can hear it. Tips for listeningThe sound files on these pages are in .wav format. If your browser can't play them back, you will need to install a plugin that will do this. The sounds on these pages require a good loudspeaker. If your computer doesn't have good speakers (a subwoofer is best) then try using a pair of earphones. I did it so hard by myself. Today we're going to talk about something called root mean squared error (RMSE), which is a fancy name for a way to see how close predictions are to the actual values. Imagine you have a weather app on your phone that tries to predict the temperature every day. Sometimes, the prediction will be exactly right, like when it says it will be 70 degrees outside and it really is 70 degrees. But other times, the prediction might be too high or too low - maybe it says it will be 75 degrees, but actually it's only 65 degrees. To figure out how well the weather app is doing overall, we need a way to add up all those differences between the predicted temperatures and the real temperatures. We could just add them all up, but that wouldn't be fair because sometimes the difference is positive (when the prediction is too high) and sometimes it's negative (when the prediction is too low). So instead, we take the square of each difference, which makes everything positive and gets rid of any minus signs. Then, we find the average of all those squares by adding them up and dividing by the number of predictions we made. Finally, we take the square root of that average to get back to our original units. And voila - that's the root mean squared error! One important thing to remember about RMSE is that it depends on the scale of the things being measured. That means it doesn't make sense to compare the RMSE of two different apps that predict completely different things, like one that predicts temperatures and another that predicts stock prices. However, it does make sense to compare the RMSE of different apps that predict the same thing, like comparing two weather apps to see which one is better at predicting temperatures. So next time you're using a weather app (or any kind of app that makes predictions), keep in mind this idea of root mean squared error and think about how close the predictions are to reality! Hello young mathematicians! Today we're going to learn about something called "inequalities." You might already know about equals signs (=), but did you know that there are other ways to compare numbers? Let's start with the "not equal to" sign. It looks like this: . This tells us that two things are not the same. For example, if we have 5 apples and someone takes 2, we now have 3 apples. We could write it as "5 3" because 5 is not equal to 3. Now, let's talk about comparing numbers in size. Imagine you have a basket with some oranges. If your friend has more oranges than you, we can show that using the "less than" symbol, which is written as "<". So if your friend has 7 oranges and you have 4, we would write it as "4 < 7", meaning "4 is less than 7". On the flip side, if you have more oranges than your friend, then your friend has fewer oranges than you. In this case, we use the "greater than" symbol, which is written as ">". Using our orange example again, if you have 7 oranges and your friend has 4, we would write it as "7 > 4", meaning "7 is greater than 4". Sometimes, numbers can be equal or unequal depending on the situation. To account for these cases, we add another symbol - the "less than or equal to" symbol ( ) and the "greater than or equal to" symbol ( ). Going back to our orange example, if you both had the same number of oranges, say 6 each, we would write it as "6 6" or "6 6", since 6 is both equal to and less than or equal to itself. And that's all there is to understanding inequalities! Remember, just like equals signs, inequalities help us compare numbers and understand their relationships better. Keep practicing, and soon you'll become an inequality expert! I hear Arte an' Jimmy talkin' outside. Authorizing Music from iTunes to Play on Other Computers ====================================================== Are you trying to play your music library on another computer but keep encountering error messages about authorization? Don't worry - this guide will walk you through the process of authorizing your music from iTunes to play on other computers with ease. By following these steps, you can ensure that all of your devices are authorized correctly, preventing any future headaches when attempting to access your purchased content. Before we begin, let's discuss some important background information: * Apple allows up to five computers to be authorized at one time per Apple ID account. This means if you want to play your iTunes purchases on more than five computers, you must deauthorize them first (which we will cover later). * When you download or purchase an item from the iTunes Store, it is automatically associated with your Apple ID. However, playing such content requires explicit permission known as "authorization." Thus, even though you own the files, they cannot be played without proper authentication. * Make sure you have administrative access to both the source and destination computers to perform these actions smoothly. **Step 1: Open iTunes on Your Source Computer** ----------------------------------------------- Launch iTunes on the original computer where you initially bought or downloaded the music. It is crucial to start here because only then can you transfer those rights to other machines. ### Key Tips * If you cannot locate the iTunes application, search for 'iTunes' using Spotlight Search (Mac) or Cortana / Start Menu (Windows). * Keep your Apple ID credentials handy since you may need them during the procedure. </s> **Step 2: Access Account Information** ------------------------------------- Click on the user profile icon located near the top right corner of the screen and select 'Account Info.' You might be prompted to enter your password again for security reasons; go ahead and do so. ![Access Account Information](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Authorize-Music-from-iTunes-to-Play-on-Other-Computers- Kink, gong, bank, gone, kink, kink, gone, kink. Title: How to Add Text in Adobe Premiere Introduction: Adobe Premiere is a powerful video editing software that offers various tools and features to enhance your videos. One such feature is the ability to add text to your videos. This can be useful for adding captions, creating opening credits, or incorporating informational overlays. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to add text in Adobe Premiere. Step 1: Open Your Project in Adobe Premiere Start by launching Adobe Premiere and open the project you want to edit. If you haven't created a project yet, do so by clicking on "New Project" under the Start screen. Step 2: Navigate to the "Title" Window Once your project is open, navigate to the "Title" window. You can find this under the "Window" menu at the top of the screen, or by using the keyboard shortcut T. The Title window allows you to create new title graphics for your video. Step 3: Create a New Title Click on "New Title" at the bottom right corner of the Title window. A dialog box will appear asking you to name your new title and select a size. For standard HD video, choose "1920 x 1080." Once you've made your selections, click "OK." Step 4: Design Your Text You'll now be taken to the Title Designer where you can customize your text. To add text, click on the "T" icon in the toolbar and then click anywhere within the workspace. Type your desired text into the text field that appears. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Use the Character Formatting panel to adjust the font, size, color, and other attributes of your text. - Utilize the Paragraph Formatting options to control alignment, leading (space between lines), indents, etc. - Experiment with different looks by changing the background color, opacity, or adding effects from the Effects tab. Step 5: Animate Your Text (Optional) If you wish to animate your text, stay in the Title Designer. Select your text layer, then go to the "Animation" tab. Here, you can set keyframes for parameters like position, scale, rotation, and opacity. By setting multiple keyframes, you can create complex animations. Step 6: Save Your Title As a Template After designing your text, save it as a template for future use. Click on "Save Preset" at the bottom left corner of the Title window. Name your preset and check the box next to "Store in Current Project," then click "OK." Step 7: Insert Your Title Into the Timeline Return to your main sequence by closing the Title window. Now, drag and drop your saved title from the Project Panel onto the Video Track of your timeline. Position it appropriately over your footage. Step 8: Preview Your Work Press the space bar to preview your edited clip complete with the added text. Make any necessary adjustments either to the timing of when the text appears or its appearance itself. Conclusion: Adding text to your videos in Adobe Premiere opens up numerous possibilities for enhancing storytelling, providing information, and engaging viewers. With patience and practice, mastering these steps will allow you to fully utilize this versatile tool. Shut the TV off your dinner's going to be ready in just a minute. Dragon Boat Festival is celebrated to commemorate the death of Qu Yuan, a patriotic poet of the State of Chu during the Warring States period. He persuaded his emperor not to accept Qin's diplomats' offers several times but his emperor did not listen to him. He was very sad and ended up jumping into the river to end his life. The people loved him so much that they did not want the fish to eat his corpse. They made and threw rice dumplings into the river. They hope the fish eat these dumplings instead of the poet's corpse. They also rowed dragon boats in the river to get rid of the fish. Such practices, eating rice dumplings and holding dragon boat races, become what Chinese do in this festival nowadays. Science Fair Project Encyclopedia The starting point for much of the action on the field is home plate, which is a white rubber pentagon seventeen inches wide. Next to each of the two parallel sides is a batter's box. The point of the pentagon is at one corner of a ninety-foot square. The other three corners of the square, in counterclockwise order from home plate, are called first base, second base, and third base. Three canvas bags fifteen inches (38 cm) square mark the three bases. These three bags along with home plate form the four bases at the corners of the infield. A little-known fact about the bases is that first and third bases are entirely within the ninety-foot square, as is home plate, but second base is not. Second base is placed so that its center coincides exactly with the corner of the ninety-foot square. The lines from home plate to first and third bases are extended infinitely and are called the foul lines. The quarter of the universe between the foul lines is fair territory; the other three-quarters of the universe is foul territory. The area in the vicinity of the square formed by the bases is called the infield; fair territory outside the infield is the outfield. Most baseball fields are enclosed with a fence that marks the outer edge of the outfield. The fence is usually set at a distance ranging from 300 to 410 feet (90 to 125 m) from home plate. Most professional and college baseball fields have a right and left foul pole. These poles are at the intersection of the foul lines and the respective ends of the outfield fence. Each pole itself is in fair territory. Any ball hitting a foul pole above the top of the outfield fence is a home run, regardless of where the ball goes after striking this pole. Foul poles are not shown on the diagram seen above. Each pole is much higher than the top of the outfield fence and serves to prove that a ball going over the fence was fair (a home run) or foul (out of play). In the middle of the square is a low mound called the pitcher's mound. There is a rubber plate, called the pitcher's rubber, six inches (15 cm) wide and two feet (61 cm) long, on the mound, exactly sixty feet six inches (18.4 m) from home plate. This peculiar distance was set due to a clerical error. When it was decided that the distance from home plate to the pitcher's mound should be increased from 50 feet to 60 feet (15.2 to 18.3 m) from the point of home plate, the builders read the diagram's notation of 60'00" as 60'06". A baseline is the direct route-a straight line- between two adjacent bases. The basepath is the region within three feet (0.9 meters) of the baseline. Baserunners are not required to run in this objective basepath, however; a baserunner may run wherever he wants when no play is being attempted on him. At the moment the defense begins to attempt a tag on him, his running baseline is established as a direct line from his current position to the base which he is trying for. The runner may not stray three feet away from this line in an attempt to avoid a tag; if he does, he is automatically out. The grass line, where the dirt of the infield ends and the grass of the outfield begins, has no special significance to the rules of the game. Its only purpose is to act as a visual aid so that participants, fans, and umpires may better judge distance from the center of the diamond. They are backed with magnets.] Title: How to Perform a Leg Mount Performing a leg mount is an essential skill in various sports and activities such as martial arts, wrestling, or even dance. It involves securing a stable position by wrapping one's legs around another person or object while maintaining balance and control. This guide will provide you with clear, concise steps to execute this move safely and effectively. **Things You'll Need:** * Flexibility and strength, particularly in your core, hips, and legs * Comfortable clothing that allows for easy movement * A partner (optional), preferably someone experienced who can offer guidance and feedback ### **Step 1: Preparation** Before attempting any physical maneuver, ensure you are properly prepared. Engage in warm-up exercises to increase blood flow and reduce the risk of injury. Focus on stretches targeting your hip flexors, glutes, hamstrings, and lower back. These areas play crucial roles during a leg mount. ### **Step 2: Understanding Body Mechanics** To successfully perform a leg mount, understanding body mechanics is vital. The primary goal is to create leverage using your legs while keeping your upper body light and agile. By doing so, you become harder to dislodge and gain better control over your opponent or the object being mounted. ### **Step 3: Positioning** Start by standing close to the individual or object you intend to mount. Your feet should be shoulder-width apart for stability. Bend at your knees slightly; this lowers your center of gravity making it easier to lift your legs. ### **Step 4: Launching into the Mount** Explosively jump towards your intended target, extending both arms simultaneously. As you leap, tuck your heels near your buttocks, preparing to wrap them around your objective. Keep your spine straight throughout this motion to avoid straining your back. ### **Step 5: Securing the Mount** Once airborne, swiftly bring your bent legs upwards, aiming to hook them just above the waistline of your partner or the edge of the surface if it's an immovable object. Make sure to maintain pressure with your thighs to prevent yourself from slipping off. ### **Step 6: Adjustments** After establishing the initial mount, make necessary adjustments for optimal comfort and security. Scoot forward until you feel firmly seated against your chosen anchor point. Distribute your weight evenly across your limbs to minimize strain and maximize longevity in the hold. ### **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Practice regularly to build muscle memory and improve technique * Always prioritize safety when training. Use protective gear where appropriate * Listen to constructive criticism from trainers or partners to refine your skills * Consider taking classes or workshops led by certified instructors for professional guidance By following these steps meticulously and adhering to our advice, you're well on your way to mastering the leg mount! Remember, consistency is key - keep practicing, stay patient, and enjoy the journey. 'Ah!' she said, 'there are more ways than one of knowing many things that are to come. In the past few years, Poland's population has gone down because of an increase in emigration and a sharp drop in the birth rate. In 2006, the census office estimated the total population of Poland at 38,536,869, a very small rise on the 2002 figure of 38,230,080. Since Poland's accession to the European Union, many Polish people have moved to work in Western European countries like the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland. Some organizations state people have left because of high unemployment (10.5%) and better opportunities for work somewhere else. In April 2007, the Polish population of the United Kingdom had risen to about 300,000 people and estimates predict about 65,000 Polish people living in the Republic of Ireland. However, in recent years strong growth of Polish economy and increasing value of Polish currency (PLN) makes many Polish immigrants to go back home. In 2007, the number of people leaving the country was lower than people who are coming back. Poland became an attractive place to work for people from other countries (mainly Ukraine). [Nina puts a wooden man on the stove] Licking Our Lips With L Rationale: This lesson will help children identify /l/, the phoneme represented by L. Students will learn to recognize /l/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation (licking their lips) and the letter symbol L, practice finding /l/ in words, and apply phoneme awareness with /l/ in phonetic cue reading. Materials: chart paper with tongue twister written on it, primary paper, pencils, chart paper with Lizards in a Log, plain white paper, assessment worksheet 1. Say: Our written language is a secret code. The tricky part is learning what letters stand for -the mouth moves we make as we say words. Today we're going to work on spotting the mouth move /l/. We spell /l/ with the letter L. The letter L looks like where your pointer finger and thumb come together, and /l/ sounds like licking your lips. 2. Say: Let's pretend to lick our lips, /l/, /l/, /l/. [Pantomime licking lips] Notice what your tongue does? The tongue starts on the roof of the mouth behind the front teeth. The tongue sometimes rolls down at the end of the /l/ sound for certain words. If the /l/ sound is at the end of the word, your tongue will start resting on your bottom teeth and then roll up to the roof of your mouth. Your mouth is also open for both /l/ sounds. 3. Say: Now I want to show you how to find the /l/ sound in the word like. I am going to say the word and stretch it out as slow as I can and you are going to listen for the "licking our lips" sound. Llll-iii-k-e. Let's all say it together now even slower. Lllllll-iiiiii-kk-e. Did you feel how your tongue started at the top of your mouth and rolled down? I felt it! 4. Say: Let's try a tongue twister [on chart]. "Lisa lost the large lemon for the lizard Lenny loved." Now let's say it three times together. Now let's say it again and stretch out the /l. sound at the beginning of each word. "Lllisa lllost the lllarge lllemon for the lllizard Lllenny llloved." We are going to say it one last time and this time break off the word: "/l/isa /l/ost the /l/arge /l/emon for the /l/izard /l/enny /l/oved." 5. [Pass out primary paper and pencil to each student].Say: We use the letter L to spell /l/. Let's practice writing capital and lower case letter L. I am going to write capital and lowercase L on the board. To write a capital L you "pull down a line and add a lap. Lie down, lazy! It's time for a nap!" I want everyone to write a capital L. After I come by and put a star next to it, I want you to make ten more just like it. Once students are done introduce lowercase letter L. While writing a lower case L on the board say "little l looks like a number one. Just draw a line and you are done!" Next I want everyone to write a lowercase L. After I come by and put a star next to it, I want you to make five more just like it. 6. Say: Now I am going to say some words and I want you to tell me if you hear the /l/ sound. [Call on students to answer and tell how they knew]. Do you hear /l/ in lake or ocean? Lamp or fan? Ball or bat? Candle or fire? Let's see if you can see my mouth move in some /l/ words. Lick your lips if you hear /l/: The, little, lamb, walked, slowly, up, the, hill. 7. Say: Let's sing a song called "Lizards in a Log". [Display the lyrics on chart paper]. [Teacher should sing first]. Five lizards live in a log. (Hold up five fingers cover them with other hand) One left to live with a frog (Fold thumb down) One left to live with a dog. (fold index finger down) Two left to live with a hog (Fold middle and ring finger down.) One little lizard living in the bog (Fold little finger.) Is a little lonely living in a log (Make a sad face.) Have children join in and sing the song three times. 8. [Pass out plain white paper to each student]. Say: On your piece of paper, I want you to draw five lips. Think of words that have the /l/ sound. Inside each drawing of lips, I want you to a word with the /l/ sound. [Students are encouraged to use inventive spelling]. [Once each student is done, have them share their /l/ words with the class. 9. For the assessment, have students color the pictures that begin with the letter sound /l/ on the activity sheet. Resource: Lick Your Lollipop with L by Kelly Meyer http://www.auburn.edu/academic/education/reading_genie/awakenings/meyerel.htm Assessment Worksheet: http://www.tlsbooks.com/letterl_1.pdf Return to Epiphanies Index Now okay so you did a lot of time you spent a lot of time with this lady. An example of fair use is when newspaper writers quote several sentences from a copyright-protected document to tell the story. Another example of "fair use" is when a university professor quotes several sentences from a copyright-protected book in a review of the book, or in a research report. Do you feel like you're going to throw up? Adjusting gravity inCounter-Strike can add an exciting twist to your gameplay experience. By manipulating the game's physics engine, you can create unique scenarios that challenge even the most experienced players. This guide will walk you through the process of adjusting gravity in CS:GO using console commands, providing detailed explanations along the way. **Step 1:** **LaunchCS:GO and Enable Developer Console** Firstly, launch CS:GO from your Steam library or any other desktop shortcut you have created. Once you are at the main menu screen, navigate to 'Settings', then select the 'Game Settings' tab. Scroll down until you see the 'Enable Developer Console' option; ensure this is set to 'Yes'. The developer console allows users access to various advanced features within the game. ![Enable Developer Console](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Enable-Developer-Console-in-CS-Go-Step-2.jpg/aid4598603/personal1.webp) **Step 2:** **Access the Developer Console** To open the developer console during gameplay, press the tilde (~) key located just below the ESC key on standard QWERTY keyboards. If pressing the tilde key does not work, check if another program (like a chat application) has claimed this key as its hotkey. You may need to change those settings accordingly. **Step 3:** **Understand Game Units and Gravity Scales** In CS:GO, distances are measured in units called 'game units.' One unit corresponds roughly to one foot in real life. Similarly, gravitational force is represented by a value known as the 'gravity scale.' A default gravity scale setting of 800 means that objects fall towards the ground with a strength similar to Earth's gravity. Changing the gravity scale alters how fast entities drop when thrown or shot into the air. **Step 4:** **Set New Gravity Scale Value** Type `mp_gravitation <value>` into the console and replace `<value>` with your desired new gravity scale number. For example, typing `mp_gravitation 400` would halve the current gravity force, making jumps last twice as long and grenades travel much further than usual. Remember, higher values increase gravity while lower ones decrease it. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Be mindful of balance: While altering gravity can be fun, excessive changes might make the game less enjoyable for others. Try testing different settings privately before introducing them to multiplayer matches. * Keep safety in mind: Lowered gravity could potentially cause motion sickness or discomfort due to rapid movement. Take regular breaks if needed. * Experiment wisely: Modifying gravity affects all aspects of game physics, including player movements, weapon behavior, and object interactions. Test out several combinations to find what suits your playstyle best. By following these steps and keeping our key tips in mind, you now possess the knowledge necessary to tweak gravity settings in CS:GO. Enjoy exploring new possibilities within the game! Title: Encourage Preschoolers to Use Their Sensory Imagination Introduction: Imaginative play is crucial for preschoolers' cognitive development as it enhances their creativity, problem-solving skills, language development, and emotional understanding. This tutorial will focus on encouraging preschoolers to utilize their sensory imagination, which involves engaging all five senses (touch, taste, sight, smell, and hearing) during imaginative activities. Here are some steps, tips, and guidelines to help you foster an environment that promotes sensory imagination in preschoolers. Step 1: Provide a Variety of Open-Ended Materials Explanation: Offer materials with different textures, colors, shapes, sizes, weights, and patterns. These objects can be natural or manmade, such as rocks, leaves, sand, water, clay, paint, blocks, fabric scraps, musical instruments, etc. The variety encourages children to explore and manipulate these items using their senses, sparking curiosity and imagination. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Rotate the materials periodically to maintain interest and encourage exploration of new things. * Introduce seasonal items like pinecones, seashells, or snow for added sensory experiences related to changing environments. * Make sure the materials are safe and age-appropriate. Step 2: Create Sensory Stations Explanation: Set up designated areas where preschoolers can engage in sensory-rich activities. For example, create a messy play station with non-toxic paints, playdough, or slime; establish a quiet corner filled with soft cushions, blankets, and books; build a sound wall with various rhythm instruments or everyday household items. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Clearly define boundaries for each station to prevent chaos and confusion. * Supervise closely when necessary, especially if potentially hazardous materials are involved. * Allow enough space for multiple children to participate simultaneously without interference. Step 3: Integrate Storytelling into Playtime Explanation: Narrate stories involving imaginary scenarios that require sensory engagement. Describe smells, sounds, tastes, textures, and visual details to stimulate preschoolers' imaginations. Encourage them to contribute ideas and expand upon the narrative using their own sensory perceptions. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Choose age-appropriate themes based on preschoolers' interests and abilities. * Ask open-ended questions to prompt further thinking and creative responses. * Model descriptive language and vocabulary to enhance communication skills. Step 4: Engage in Role-Play Activities Explanation: Facilitate role-play scenarios that involve using all five senses. For instance, set up a pretend bakery where kids can knead dough (touch), listen to mixing sounds (hearing), observe various ingredients (sight), smell baking goods (smell), and even taste samples (taste). Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be prepared with props, costumes, and background noises to enrich the experience. * Encourage cooperation and sharing among peers during group role-plays. * Respect individual preferences and provide alternative options for those who may feel uncomfortable participating in certain roles. Step 5: Explore Nature Together He seemed to know some of the animal names too like when you would point them out to him. Title: How to Add a Filter to an iPhone Screen Introduction: Adding filters to your iPhone screen can help enhance its appearance and customize it according to your personal preferences. This tutorial will guide you through the process of adding a filter to your iPhone screen using the built-in Accessibility features. By following these steps, you'll be able to improve visibility, reduce eye strain, or simply add a unique touch to your device. Step 1: Enable Reduce White Point 1. Open the 'Settings' app on your iPhone home screen. 2. Scroll down and tap on 'Accessibility'. 3. Tap on 'Display & Text Size'. 4. Locate 'Reduce White Point' and toggle the switch to enable this feature. 5. Adjust the slider to set the level of intensity that suits your needs. Explanation: Enabling Reduce White Point reduces the intensity of bright colors on your screen, making them less harsh on your eyes. It is particularly helpful when using your phone in low light conditions or if you have sensitivity to bright screens. The adjustable slider allows you to fine-tune the effect based on your preference and requirements. Key Tips: * Experiment with different intensities to find the right balance between visual comfort and color accuracy. * Remember that enabling Reduce White Point may slightly darken the overall display, so ensure adequate lighting when using this feature. Step 2: Apply Color Filters (Optional) 1. Go back to the 'Display & Text Size' menu in Accessibility settings. 2. Scroll down to 'Color Filters'. 3. Toggle the switch to enable Color Filters. 4. Choose from predefined filters like Grayscale, Red/Green, Blue/Yellow, etc., or create a Custom Filter by tapping 'Color Tints'. 5. Use the hue and intensity sliders to further tweak the filter as needed. Explanation: Applying Color Filters alters the entire color palette displayed on your iPhone screen. These filters are beneficial for users who need assistance distinguishing certain colors due to visual impairments such as protanopia, deuteranopia, tritanopia, or other forms of color blindness. Additionally, grayscale mode can aid in focusing on content rather than being distracted by vivid colors. Key Guidelines: * When selecting a predefined filter, choose one that best accommodates your specific vision needs. * If creating a Custom Filter, consider consulting with a professional familiar with your condition for optimal results. Conclusion: By following this tutorial, you've successfully added a filter to your iPhone screen using the built-in Accessibility features. With Reduce White Point enabled and potentially combined with Color Filters, you now have improved control over your device's display, tailoring it to meet your individual preferences and visual requirements. Enjoy exploring new ways to customize and protect your eyes while using your iPhone! Joseph, you may not have the screwdriver. Turn it over and say the magic words. Rivera Elementary School Environmental Education Team 6. Natural Resources 8. Attitudes, Values 1. Language Arts 3. Social Studies By using their five senses, children will make observations and correctly classify some items found in a natural area into living and non-living in their present state. (Ex. leaf on a tree is living; leaf fallen from a tree is non-living.) To better appreciate nature, children may begin at an early age to experience and learn about the way it functions. The uniqueness of nature can be experienced through seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, and tasting. By this activity, children will learn to make simple classification of what things are living or non-living, and what non-living things are associated with the source from which they came. Supplies needed: blindfolds, cardboard writing surface, white paper, and crayons. Students should be divided into groups of five, and with adult, move from station to station. Each of these activities can be done separately or in combination at each place. Teacher or leader should read activity directions at each place. On the walk with our small group we will make frequent stops. At these times, we will stress observing with one of the senses. A. Feeling Time Sit in a small circle in your wild place. Put on your blindfold. Your teacher will find things to pass around, one at a time. Feel them with your hand, and say one thing about it, and also decide if it is dead or alive. Listen to each student's description. After everything has been passed around, take off blindfolds and look at the things. 1. How many were dead or living things? 2. Who had the best descriptions? The funniest? 3. The most creative? The most imaginative? B. Listening Time At this spot, everyone must be very quiet as they sit in a circle again with blindfolds on. Listen for 5-10 minutes to the sounds of wind, and bushes, and trees; of animals and birds; of insects or bugs; and other sounds. (If you have a tape recorder--try to capture these sounds). What sounds are coming from living things? From dead things? Can you tell what is making these sounds? Use words to describe the sounds you hear. Have each student share this information on any sound with the others. C. Smelling Time At this station, again with blindfolds on, smell things that are passed around by your teacher. Each student, in turn describes the smell and labels it as coming from a dead or alive thing. When all things have been passed around, remove blindfolds and look at the things. How many came from dead things? From live things? Who had the D. Looking Time At this 'Location, 'look around and try to describe your wild place as other students listen. Try to be imaginative and accurate. Pick out one special thing that you 'Like the most about your wild place. Tell about it to the others; tell whether it is living or dead. What makes it a living or dead thing? E. At this wild place, find something to make a crayon rubbing. Place the paper you have over the thing, and using a crayon, rub over the surface to make an impression. Was your thing living? Dead7 Take this back to school and make a display for others to see. F. Tasting Time NOTE--TASTE ONLY SAFE THINGS--grass, sugar cane slice, sour sop leaf, etc. Again, use blindfolds, and tell what each thing seems to be. Is it dead or alive? When everyone has finished, take off blindfolds and see how you did! G. Thinking Time Here, you stop to sit quietly for awhile and think about your wild place. 1. Is it good to be here? 2. How do you feel about yourself today? 3. Would you like to come back? 4. Would you like to be an animal in this place? 5. Would you like to be a plant in this place? 6. Would you want someone to change this place? 7. How would you feel if there was junk here? 8. How would you keep this place nice? If there is time left, you may just wish to sit and chat, play a game, act out some characters or animals, or anything else you can think of. Sarah what's the name of those shoes? In one experiment, Lavoisier found that there was no increase in weight when tin and air were heated in a closed container. He also found that air rushed in when the container was opened. After this, he found that the weight of the tin had increased by the same amount as the weight of the air that rushed in. He published his findings in 1777. He wrote that air was made up of two gases. One he called "vital air" (oxygen), which is needed for combustion and respiration. The other (nitrogen) he called "azote", which means "lifeless" in Greek. (This is still the name of nitrogen in some languages, including French.) No but you get the sort of feeling that God is visiting you. Hello young learners! Today, let's talk about a fun concept called "the absolute value of a number." You may already know about adding and multiplying numbers, but today we'll explore something extra special about multiplying two complex numbers together. Imagine you have two secret codes, like "7i" and "3i", where i represents the square root of -1. Now, these are not ordinary numbers; they belong to a magical world of complex numbers! To keep things simple, think of them as vectors on a graph, starting from zero and pointing in different directions. When you multiply these two secret codes, just like regular numbers, you follow these steps: 1. Multiply their distances from zero (which are 7 and 3), giving us 21. 2. Add the angles they make with the positive real axis (imagine a clock face, where 0 or 360 points towards the right, 90 points upwards, etc.). Let's assume our codes make an angle of 90 each. So, when we multiply, we add those angles, resulting in 180 . But wait! We want this answer back in our magical complex world. This is where the absolute value comes in handy. In our example, since both codes had distance 1 from zero (meaning they were on the unit circle), we simply take the product of the distances (still 21) and say that the result has an absolute value of 21. But remember, we added angles and got 180 ? That becomes the argument of our final code - the direction it makes with the positive real axis. Our final answer would then be written as `21 * (-i)` because -i lies 180 degrees anticlockwise from the positive real axis. Now, coming back to Lean and your question, mathematicians love writing down rules that connect concepts. They discovered a cool pattern involving complex conjugate, multiplication, and the absolute value. The rule says that for any complex number Z, its conjugate times Z equals the square of its absolute value! Isn't that amazing? It looks like this: `Z* ~Z = |Z|^2`. In the code snippet provided, someone was trying to figure out exactly this rule using Lean, but they weren't sure how to switch between a complex number and "is\_R\_or\_C", another mathematical creature helping us compare properties of real and complex worlds. Thankfully, Lean experts showed them an easier way by suggesting to use `complex.norm_sq_eq_conj_mul_self`, a built-in function that directly proves the rule without having to switch worlds. How clever! To summarize, exploring the connections between operations in mathematics leads to exciting discoveries like the relationship between complex conjugates, multiplication, and absolute values. And even though some ideas might seem complicated initially, learning communities like Lean users always find ways to simplify and share their knowledge through easy-to-use functions and tools. [question prosody on first and second clauses] " So what do you eat with your lox? " [Nina takes a little package out of the stocking] Breathing is something we do without even thinking about it. But some people have a hard time breathing easily. They wheeze, cough, and just can't get enough air into their lungs. Let's talk about asthma. Normally when you breathe, oxygen-rich air enters your nose and mouth and travels to the airways in your lungs. But when you have an asthma attack, the muscles in your airways tighten. Your airways swell up and get narrower. It's like pressing on a straw when you're trying to drink through it. The more you press, the less of your drink can squeeze up through the straw. In the same way, less air can squeeze through tight airways into your lungs. So, what causes asthma? People with asthma have different triggers. Some people are sensitive to pet hair or dander. Others find that dust, pollen, smoke, or chemicals make them wheeze. You may have trouble breathing when you're under stress or working out at the gym. Often people with asthma have allergies that trigger their attacks. Others have a parent or other relative who has allergies. You may ask, ow can you know for sure that you have asthma? During an asthma attack, you'll have trouble breathing. You may cough or wheeze as you try to draw air into your lungs. If you're having a severe attack, your lips and face may turn blue and you'll have a hard time getting any air. That's when it's time to call for emergency help. Your doctor will listen to your lungs with a stethoscope, and may do other tests to check your blood and lung function. To find out what's triggering your asthma, you may need to be tested for allergies to mold, pollen, pet dander, or other substances. To measure the strength of air flowing out of your lungs, you'll blow into a device called a peak flow meter. You can also use a peak flow meter to make sure you're keeping your asthma under control. Different medicines are used to prevent and treat asthma. Control drugs help you avoid asthma attacks, but you need to take them every day for them to work. You breathe in control drugs like Flovent, Singulair, and Pulmicort through an inhaler. If you're having an asthma attack, you can take a quick-relief drug to control your wheezing. Examples of quick-relief drugs include short-acting inhaled drugs and steroid medicines that you take by mouth. When you have asthma, you need to be prepared. Know your asthma triggers and try to avoid them. Carry an inhaler with you in case you have an asthma attack. If you ever have an attack that's so severe you can't breathe, call 911 or get emergency medical help. [places the fifth silver man at the top of the pyramid] I see a building and de baby. Since the 20th century there have been two main types of astronomy, "observational" and "theoretical" astronomy. Observational astronomy uses telescopes and cameras to "observe" or look at stars, galaxies and other astronomical objects. Theoretical astronomy uses maths and computer models to explain the observations and predict what might happen. Working together, theories predict what should happen and observations show whether the predictions work. The main work of astronomy is to explain puzzling features of the universe. For thousands of years the most important issue was the motions of planets; now many other topics are studied. But such a belief was the rule in Don Alphonso's day, and when it could lay so strong a hold upon grown men and women, it would, of course, be likely to work in peculiar ways with thoughtful and conscientious children, who, understanding little of the real meaning of sacrifice and penance, felt it their duty to do something as proof of their belief. When starch is eaten, the human body breaks it apart into smaller sugars. An enzyme is added in the mouth, but it only begins to work in the stomach. Pure starch is actually tasteless in the mouth. It gave the first data on the structure of a comet nucleus and how the "coma" (nebulous envelope around the core or nucleus) and the tail formed. These observations supported Fred Whipple's "dirty snowball" model. This correctly predicted that Halley would be composed of a mixture of volatile ices - such as water, carbon dioxide, ammonia, - and dust. The missions also adjusted these ideas. For example, it is now known that the "surface" of Halley is mostly dusty, non-volatile materials, and that only a small portion of it is icy. But I don't think y' understand nothin' huh. It is difficult to give an exact definition for "jazz". A singer Nina Simone said, "Jazz is not just music, it is a way of life, it is a way of being, a way of thinking". But when we talk about jazz as music, one important part of jazz is improvisation (improv), which means the person playing is making music up as they go along. If a jazz band is playing a song, the song may have several solos where one player will improvise while the rest of the band, except for the rhythm section (such as the piano, bass, or drums), does not play. "For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them." Eph. 2:10, KJV A point is a location, and a dot is a mark at a certain point. Dots are the building blocks of all the other elements. Dots give meaning to a canvas. They provide points of focused attention and reference points. When God created us he put a "dot" in our hearts, a conscious, and knowledge of him. He gives us a reason for living. 4. Print out the Bible Verse, Element of Design, and Bible Lesson Summary Page (KJV or NIV). Cut out the labels and glue them to the inside folder on the fold-out sides. Save room on one side for the Dot Itty Bitty Book if you decide to make one. (Printing Problems?) Preparation: You will need paper, crayon, markers, water color paint, and other artistic media. (Hand out blank pieces of paper to your students. Have markers, pencils, crayons, and paint available on the table.) What do you think of when you look at the piece of paper in front of you? (Children might answer: It's blank, there's nothing on it, it's boring, can we write on it, can we put our names on it, or it needs something.) A blank piece of paper isn't very interesting, but there are some things that are very important on your piece of paper that you can't see. Your piece of paper is filled with points. If you place your finger on your paper, you are touching a point on that paper. A point is a location. Picture a point on your paper. It may be in the center, on the bottom, left-hand side, or at the very top of your paper. A dot is a mark at a specific point on your paper. A dot can be any size, color (colors) or shape. Picture a dot in your mind, look at all the different things you have in front of you to make a dot. Decide what you would like to use to make the dot and how big you want to make the dot. Once you have decided what you want to use, go ahead and make one dot at the point you picked on your paper. (Let your students show off their creations. Ask each student why he or she chose to place his or her dot where he or she did, and why he or she picked a certain color, point, or size for his or her dot.) Each of you just created a picture of a dot on a point or points. Some are more interesting then others, some took more thought and imagination, but they are all different. Your dot picture reveals something about you. Placing that dot on the piece of paper gave that piece of paper a purpose. It gave it meaning. A dot on a piece of paper gives you something to focus on and think about. The dot on your paper made it much more interesting, didn't it? When you see a dot on a piece of paper you begin to ask yourself questions about it. You might wonder why it is there, why there aren't more, why it is so small or so big, or who made the dot? You wonder what's the point. A dot gives a piece of paper meaning. It's a starting point. The dot says, "Look at me. I'm here for a reason. Someone created me." You are a lot like this blank piece of paper when you don't have God in your heart. You fill empty and alone. But God has put a dot in your heart. He has given you the knowledge of him, a sense of right and wrong, or a point of reference. You know somehow that there has to be a point to your existence. When you recognize that dot inside you you recognize the creator of the "dot" and are drawn to him. God opens your eyes and you can see other dots and soon a picture appears, a beautiful picture of your life with a purpose and future. Without Christ you are lost, but God loved you so much he sent his son to die for your sins and pay your debt. Through his grace you were (can be) saved. When you listen to that voice that tells you there is a purpose for your life and that you were created by a great designer, your life begins to have meaning, just like a blank sheet of paper begins to have a purpose when an artist places a dot on it. You are like a work of art. Paul tells us in Ephesians, "For we are God's workmanship, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do." Eph. 2:10 Some Bible versions use the word "masterpiece" instead of workmanship. When you follow Jesus and do what you were created to do you become what our creator intended you to be, a masterpiece! When God created you, he had a plan for you, he wants you to be happy, he wants to give you hope and a future. You can only find that through Jesus. "For I know the plans I have for you," declares the LORD, "plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you hope and a future." "Since what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them. For since the creation of the world God's invisible qualities-his eternal power and divine nature-have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made, so that people are without excuse." Romans 1:19-20 The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies proclaim the work of his hands. Day after day they pour forth speech; night after night they display knowledge. There is no speech or language where their voice is not heard. Their voice goes out into all the earth, their words to the ends of the world which is like a bridegroom coming forth from his pavilion, like a champion rejoicing to run his course. It rises at one end of the heavens and makes its circuit to the other; nothing is hidden from its heat."Psalm 19:1-6 An artist is a lot like God in that his creations reveal who he is, or his character. When the artist places a mark on a blank page he is giving the page meaning and revealing something about himself. An artist creates because he loves to create. He gets pleasure from his work. God created you for his pleasure and he wants to have a relationship with you. Help us to honor you by our actions. Help us to remember that you created us to do good works and to help others. Amen This is a great book for children who think they have no artistic ability! Vashti spends her whole art class sitting in a chair doing nothing because she thinks she can't draw. At the end of the class her teacher studies her blank paper and declares, "Ah! A polar bear in a snow storm." Vashti isn't amused and complains, "I just can't draw!" Her art teacher encourages her to "Just make a mark and see where it takes you", and that is exactly what she does. She grabs a big black marker and jabs it onto the middle of the paper and hands it to her teacher. The next day she discovers that her teacher framed her picture and hung it in the front of the class. Looking at the framed picture Vashti decides she can make an even better dot, and starts what becomes a whole series of dots, and a new confidence in her artistic abilities. Have your children experiment to see how many different kinds of dots they can make using different artistic mediums. When their pictures have dried have them pick out their favorites and frame them. You can use the frame patterns below or buy mat boards. Make sure they sign their names to their artwork. 2. Learn About Pointillism and Artist George Seurat Show your children the print out of the description of pointillism and the article about artist George Seurat (See preparations) to your children, and follow the links to see pictures of Seurat's paintings. (Printing Problems?) George Seurat was a French painter who developed the artistic technique called pointillism. Pointillism uses only small dots of pure color to make a complete pictures. The close positioning of the dots fool the eye into seeing the dots as a mix of the different colors of dots (or a different color). For example, if a yellow dot is next to a red dot, the eye, at a certain distance, will see the combination of the two dots as orange. George Seurat spent two years painting his famous painting, "A Sunday Afternoon". 3. Dots are Used in Many Other Types of Art 1. Pointillism withBottle Caps. 2. Cross Stitch - Close up picture of a Cross Stitch Picture. 3. Computer image editing programs use dots of color. The squares are so small that it is hard to distinguish between them making it look like a solid color or gradual change in color. Close up of Pistil Area 4. Decorate a Bible Verse Sheet Dots Print out the Bible Verse Sheet onto colorful paper. Have your children decorate the sheet using small dots with markers, paint, or colored glue. Add tempera paint to the glue and have your children squeeze dots onto the paper. 5. Make Pictures Using Dotters and Discuss Good Works Print out the "Created to do Good Works!" Activity Sheet. Have your children think about good works they can do and write them around the border of the page. Have them decorate their pictures with dotters or larger magic markers. . Don't forget to have them sign their names to their works of art. (Printing Problems?) 6. "Dot" by Patricia Intriago - Creative Graphic Exercises "Dot" - It's amazing what you can do with a dot! Using opposites and simple modifications to the dots such as relative position, size, and color Patricia Intriago transforms dots into completely different objects or concepts. 1. Read the first couple of pages of this book and discuss with your child how each dot conveys the message on the page. Point out the fact the accompanying page gives clues to the overall concept. For example, the first page shows a red dot. It might read "Red dot.", but it says "Stop dot." The accompanying page shows a green dot. By following the concept of the description of the red dot, we know the green dot page will say, "Go dot.", not "Green dot". Read several pages of the book and discuss the concepts, and then have your child compare the pictures and see if he or she can figure out what the text might read. 2. Make your Own Itty Bitty Dot Book Using opposites, positioning, and simple lines have your children make their own dot books. When they have finished see if you can figure out what their dots represent looking at the pictures without the text. This is a great exercise for any age. Children must try to convey a message with the least amount of information possible. Younger children can use crayons and older children may want to work with a computer program that allows you to manipulate shapes. If a child's picture has too much information, ask him to experiment with deleting as much information as possible, but still maintain the message. Print out the Itty Bitty Book Pattern, cut out the pages, but don't cut them apart. For Directions on How to Assemble the Books go to the Printing Instructions Page. (Printing Problems?) 7. Learn About Aboriginal Dot Painting Follow the links below. Read the description of Aboriginal dot art from the printout to your children. Glue the description to the file folder. Videos of Aboriginal Dot Paintings Aboriginal Dot Painting - Students make dot paintings of natural forms and animals that reflects their personalities. Shows how they did a lot of pre-planning before starting their paintings. Aboriginal Artist Judy Watson Napangardi - Judy demonstrates her dot painting techniques using a series of dots to make thick lines. She makes very large paintings and sits on her canvas while she works. Aborigines originally used paint they made from natural items such as clay (Ochre) mixed with saliva, eggs, or animal fat. Children will enjoy trying to make their own paints. We used orange clay, charcoal, and pollen from sunflowers to make the natural paints used on this rock. Other things they might use are crushed fruit and vegetables such as berries and beets. Paint with sticks that have been flattened at the ends. Rub the ends on sandstone to flatten them. The Aborigines painted things that had meaning to them, to tell stories, and teach. Show your children the different symbols they used and ask them what symbols we use today. Have your children paint things that have meaning to them using symbols and dots. This lovely book lets children know that they are important and were created for a purpose.
