[words spaced out; 2.4 sec; S breathing] |guitar chords||guitar scales||scales to chords||chord progressions||glossary||songs||arpeggio||guitar licks||misc.| |chord name||reverse scales||chords to scale||metronome||forums||tuner||jam||lessons||links| Music Theory: Part II - Notes and Scales The following lesson is part two of the Music Theory lesson series. In this lesson I will cover very basic concepts such as notes on the fret board and the major and minor scales. Intermediate topics such as modes and scale structure will be included. I will also touch on variations such as seventh, augmented and diminished scales. This lesson will draw from some several of my previous lesson as well as some new material. I will add reference points throughout the lesson. Reference links will be marked with a VL. Click on the link and a new tab will open in your browser in order to view the lesson being referenced. Before we move on, it is important that you understand the layout of the fret board. A whole tone or major second is the equivalent of two consecutive semitones or half steps. An example of a whole tone movement would be moving from C to D. A semitone is a chromatic movement to an adjacent note, i.e. C to C#. In western music, we have what is called an octave. An octave is a series of twelve tones placed at half step, or half tone intervals; however, there are definitions for nineteen notes. The notes in an octave are: A A# Bb B Cb C C# Db D D# Eb E Fb F F# Gb G G# Ab. Some of these notes are harmonically equivalent. There are seven pairs of harmonically equivalent notes. These pairs are: A#-Bb, B-Cb, C#-Db, D#-Eb, E-Fb, F#-Gb and G#-Ab. Scales are generally note groups of seven notes. In some instances that number may vary, such as pentatonic scales, which are groups of five notes or the chromatic scale, which contains all twelve tones in western music. Scales are defined by intervals and not so much by the notes they contain. An interval is the distance between tones, i.e. wholes step or half step or whole tone and semitone. --(Modes are defined as scales inside of one key)-- There are seven modes in any major key. All the modes in a major key will contain the same notes, however, the notes will be placed at different intervals, or in different orders as it were, thus giving them all unique tonal qualities. The seven modes are: Outside of western music, there are, but not limited to, Persian, Arabian and Indian patterns. Indian music uses a twenty-two tone octave, while Persian and Arabian music take advantage of quarter-tones which are half the size of the semitones in western music. The Chromatic Scale The Chromatic scale is a scale which divides an entire octave into its semitones. In one octave there are twelve semitones (or thirteen if you count the root note twice), or half notes. Here is an example of the Chromatic scale: As you can see the notes in the scale are C C# D D# E F F# G G# A A# B and C. Playing in successive half steps is called playing chromatically, hence the name Chromatic scale. The Chromatic scale is the mother of all other scales and chords in western music. The Chromatic scale can be a tough concept for some guitarists out there, even some who can already shred have no clue how apply the scale. I like to think of the Chromatic scale as a tool used to enhance other scales by throwing in chromatic tones here and there. If you think about it, you play chromatics more often than you may realize. Take the A minor blues scale: In all reality, the chromatic scale is not and "must know scale". But it is a useful tool. I would say that the chromatic scale is to the guitarist as the power saw is to the construction worker. Not a necessity, but a useful tool. The Major Scale and It's Modes There are seven modes in every major key. Take C major. Each note in that key is the root note for a mode in that key. The modes in any major key, in order, are Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian and Locrian. Now, sticking with C Major, you have C Ionian (also known as the C major) as the first mode in the key. Now you go from there: You ask, what determines the order of the modes or what determines which note in a key will be the root note of a mode? The answer is simple. Each note in a key corrosponds with a degree. Degrees are the numbered order of the notes in a key/scale. So, take C major: All the modes in a major key will have the same notes. The notes will always go in order of the Alphabet. Each mode will start with the root note of that mode, i.e. D Dorian will start with D and not C even though it is in the key of C major. All of the above modes are in the key of C major, thus, they all contain the same notes. Learning how to play the modes is easy. Learning how to comprehend the modes is a little harder. I've sorted out the intverals for each mode so you can go ahead and build your own. And... Just to show you what the hell all of the above is, here is an example: Too Hot Too Touch Baby! Ok, so have you heard enough about "The major scale and all its modes"? I have reorganized the modes from the way I showed you above, just to give you another way to look at them. The Degrees of the Major Scale and its Modes Here is a diagram showing you all seven modes of a major key up and down one string. Each mode starts on the first fret and I've indicated the degrees of each scale with the numbers in parenthesis. Sweet Sweet Sevenths Using the same table as above, I've broken down seventh chords to make them easier to understand. Seven Chords are four note chords made from wierd scale degrees all in one octave. Seventh chords are very useful in creating awesome sounding arpeggios. Diminished and Augmented Triads Diminished - Flat 3rd and Flat 5th Augmented - Sharp 5th/Flat 6th Ok, now that you know a few basics lets practice some arpeggios using what I've just shown you. For this exercise we are going to play in the key of C major. Lets first take a look at the notes in C major: C D E F G A B Now lets make our arpeggios: Here they are: Lets go over one more extremely important scale for shredding; the Harmonic Minor scale. I'm going to explain the C Harmonic Minor scale: As you can see, to go from the natural minor to the harmonic minor you flat the 7th. The chord proggression for C harmonic minor is usually as follows: Well, that about sums up this lesson. The intent here was to give you a pretty good understanding of certain scales and how they work. There are many, many more scales in the universe of shred, but these, in my opinion, are the essentials. The next lesson in the series will more than likely be on chords and harmony. The lesson will also, more than likely, contain more new material than this one did. Also, if there is something I might have missed, or something you would have liked to see in this lesson or any of my lessons, don't hesitate to let me know. I am sure you have noticed a lot of repetition in this lesson. I do that because I feel it is the best way to hammer something into someones head. I hope you find these useful. Hello young mathematicians! Today we are going to talk about a special kind of math problem called a "trinomial equation." This type of equation has three parts, just like the name suggests. Have you ever seen an equation that looks something like this?: x + 3x - 10 = 0 This is an example of a quadratic equation, which is a specific type of trinomial equation. But today, we're going to learn about an even more complicated trinomial equation, one that Johann Heinrich Lambert solved way back in the 1700s. His equation looked like this: x^m + x = q In this equation, m and q are numbers, and x is the unknown number that Lambert wanted to figure out. Can you imagine trying to solve this equation without a calculator or computer? It sounds impossible, doesn't it? But Lambert found a way to do it, and we're going to explore his method together. First, let's break down the equation into smaller parts. We know that x^m means "x multiplied by itself m times," so if m were equal to 2, this part of the equation would mean "x squared." And if m were equal to 3, this part of the equation would mean "x cubed," or "x multiplied by itself 3 times." So x^m is like a super-powered version of x that changes depending on the value of m. Next, there's the "+ x" part of the equation. This just means "plus x," so our whole equation says "super-powered x plus regular x equals some number q." Our goal is to find the value of x that makes this equation true. Now, let's take a closer look at how Lambert actually solved this equation. To do this, he introduced a new variable, w, which is equal to x / (1 + x). In other words, w is the fraction that results when you divide x by (1 + x). By doing this, Lambert transformed the original trinomial equation into a new equation involving w. The next step involves a clever trick using logarithms, which are fancy mathematical functions that measure the size of numbers. Without getting too deep into the details, Lambert used logarithms to rewrite the equation again, making it easier to solve. Finally, after several more steps, Lambert arrived at a formula for finding the value of x based on the values of m and q. Even though Lambert's equation may seem very difficult, it's important to remember that great mathematicians throughout history have faced challenges just like these and found ways to overcome them. Who knows - maybe someday you will discover a new way to solve a tough math problem, just like Johann Heinrich Lambert did! But I think he understands like intonation and meaning more so. Don't whisper Chi [name and middle name] that's rude. Some of the simplest dynamical systems consist of a point moving along the number line according to a rule. For example, if we can the rule to be " given a number, reduce the number by one-half". Then if we start with an initial value of 8, after one application of the rule we get 4. After another application, we get 2, then 1, then 1/2 , 1/4, 1/8 and so on. A key goal of dynamical systems is to determine how points move as the rule is repeated over and over again. In this example, we see that the numbers keep getting smaller and they will get closer and closer to 0. Note that the values never get exactly to 0; they are always a tiny bit bigger than 0. We say that the limit of the values is 0. The concept of limit is very important in mathematics. If you play the iteration game on a calculator, after iterating the initial value 8 many times, the calculator may show the value to be exactly 0. A calculator can only keep track of a fixed number of decimal places (often 8 or 16 depending on the calculator) so when a number gets too small, the calculator thinks it is 0 when it is really not. Keep this problem in mind when you play the iteration game. Suppose we start with a different initial value (20 or -10 for example). What values do we get as we apply the rule and where do the points move towards? The Iiteration Game allows us to study this question. You can choose from one of several different rules, enter your own initial value and see its iterates. The rule is written in the form of a function f(x). For example, the halving rule would be written as f(x) = 1/2 x. Among the rules you can study are the doubling rule ( f(x) = 2x), the square root rule ( f(x) = sqrt(x)), the squaring rule ( f(x) = x^2), the sine rule ( f(x) = sin x), the cosine rule ( f(x) = cos x) , and the logistic rule f(x) = 4x(1-x). The iteration game displays the iterates as numbers. Sometimes it is easier to understand the dynamics when we look at a picture. The Staircase Game provides a pictorial way to look at iteration. Discussion of Mathematics. The game is set up to study the dynamics of the logistic family in which the rule of motion is f(x) = kx(1-x). For each k value, we get a slightly different rule of motion. Changing the k value is like changing the shape of a billiard table; it will cause the behaviour of iterates to change. We would like to see what happens for different k values. One can choose k values between 0 and 4. You can choose an initial starting point any where in the interval 0 to 1. You choose how many iterations the program will produce. For some k values, it is not clear what pattern the orbit is producing. The first few iterates move around seemingly without a pattern and it is only the later iterates that follow a clear pattern. To help in observing this type of behaviour, you can tell the program to only start drawing the orbit after a certain number of iterates have already happened. If a point does not move under the rule of motion, we call it a fixed point. For example, with the halving rule, if we start at 0 and take half of that, we still get 0. So 0 is a fixed point of the motion. Expressed in terms of the function f, a fixed point has the property that f(x) = x. There are two main types of fixed points: attracting and repelling. A fixed point is attracting if all points that start near the fixed point approach the fixed point. You can check that 0 is an attracting fixed point for the halving rule: all that start near 0 will approach it. A fixed point is repelling if points that start near it move away from it. As you play with the different rules, see if you can determine the fixed points and decide if they are attracting or repelling. f(x) = 1/2 x. The point x=0 is a fixed point and it is attracting. All other initial values will approach 0. f(x) = 2x. The fixed point is x=0. This fixed point is repelling; nearby points move away from 0. Any positive initial value will just get bigger and bigger; it moves off towards positive infinity. Any negative initial value will get bigger and bigger negative and move off towards negative infinity. Since these numbers are moving to the left along the number line, we say they are getting smaller - even though the size of the number is getting bigger! A confusing situation. f(x) = sqrt(x). Since the square root is not defined for negative numbers. There are two fixed points: 0 (repelling) and 1 (attracting). Any initial value in the interval (0,1) will approach 1. Initial values bigger than 1 will all approach 1. This example is tricky. If you start with a value bigger than 1, it will get smaller with each application of the rule. You might predict that since the values keep getting smaller, they will approach 0; this is not the case! They approach but never reach 1. f(x) = x^2. The fixed points are x=0 and x=1. The fixed point x=0 is attracting; any point that starts in the interval (-1, 1) will approach 0. The fixed point x=1 is repelling; if x is in the interval (0, 1), it goes towards 0 and away from 1. If the initial value is bigger than 1, the iterates go towards positive infinity. Note that a negative initial value becomes positive and then stays positive. f(x) = sin(x). A word of warning. This function is defined in terms of radians not degrees. The point x=0 is an attracting fixed point. All other initial points will iterate towards 0. However, they will approach 0 very, very slowly! f(x) = cos(x). Any initial point will iterate towards the value x=.739. This point is an attracting fixed point for the map. It is not easy to figure out which point solves the fixed point equation f(x) = x. f(x) = 4x(1-x). CHAOS! If we start with an initial value x in the interval (0,1), the orbit will not have any fixed pattern. The numbers will keep varying and never approach any fixed value. This is an example of chaos. There are a few points whose orbit does have a predictable behaviour. For example, x=0 is a fixed point. And x=1 will go to x=0 and then stay there. Can you find other initial values that have simple orbits? I think there's so much going on you get distracted. Swinton answered. "I'm cramped sitting here." "I'm game," Lord Victor agreed. Title: How to Appreciate Detective Conan Introduction: Detective Conan, also known as Case Closed in some regions, is a popular manga series written and illustrated by Gosho Aoyama. The story revolves around Jimmy Kudo, an amateur detective who transforms into a child after being poisoned by members of a criminal syndicate he was investigating. To avoid detection, Jimmy takes on the alias Conan Edogawa and continues solving mysteries while searching for the cure to his condition. This guide will help you appreciate this intriguing anime/manga series with its captivating stories, complex characters, and engaging themes. Instead, they come from our instincts or feelings. For example, reason does not tell us that one thing causes another. Instead, we see one thing and then we see another, and we "feel" a link between the two. Similarly, reason does not tell us that someone is a good person. Instead, we see that the person is kind and friendly, and we "feel" a special moral feeling. Because Hume thought that these beliefs do not come from reason, people call him a "skeptical" or "anti-rationalist" philosopher. You don't put it all over the place? Caring for School Pets - Hamsters Students will learn about caring for pets such as hamsters. Teachers have pets in their classrooms, so children need to learn how to care for pets. I will focus on hamsters. If you would like to have a pet in your classroom, you might think about hamsters. They are easy to care for and children like hamsters. First, you need to get a cage, a hamster, food, bedding, toys, and a water bottle. Having a glass cage with a screen top is good. Glass cages are better because the bedding stays inside the cage. The bedding and food would come out of wire cages. You need to wash out the cage and dry it. Then, you need to add the bedding and the toys such as a wheel and a little house. After that, you need to fill up the water bottle and place it into the cage. When you have everything ready, you can place your hamster in the cage and place the screen top on the cage. After you have the cage and hamster set up in the classroom, you can talk to the children about caring for the hamster. You can have the children help care for the hamster. You should feed hamsters once a day, and you should change their water bottle once a day. It is important for hamsters to have fresh food and water. You also need to clean out the cage once a week. Children like to help care for pets. Each day you can assign a student to feed the hamster and give him fresh water. It is also important to keep hamsters warm. You can also add vitamins in the water bottle. Hamsters sleep during the day, so it wouldn't make any noises while you teach. Gerbils also make good pets. They are awake during the day and sleep at night. You can consider a gerbil instead of a hamster. You would still care for gerbils like you would hamsters. Beginning Reading Design After this lesson children will better understand that when the letters e and a are put together they are pronounced /E/, and that this is called a digraph. Children will learn to identify the vowel digraph ea= /E/ in written language through various activities such as listing words that include the digraph, practicing a tongue twister, playing a game of Hangman, etc. The Deep Sea by Matt Sims Large dry erase board & marker Individual dry erase board (plastic plates will work) & markers for each student Pictures of words with ea= /E/ digraph. Pictures: leaf, peach, sea, peas, teapot, steam, seal, meal, speak, and sneakers. Chart paper containing the tongue twister, "Seals in the sea steal sneakers" Assessment sheet with pseudowords. 1. Assess the student's prior knowledge of the digraph ea. Ask the students to name some words that have the /E/ phoneme. Write the list on the board. Most likely, some of the words will contain the ea digraph; if not, include some examples: east, dream, heat, and year. I will single out the words with the ea digraph. "What do you notice about how we can spell /E/? Do all of these words spell /E/ the same way? (No). Well today we're going to look at this spelling (single out word with ea digraph.) Sometimes we can spell one phoneme, or sound, like /E/, with two different letters; this is called a digraph. 2. Next I will show the students the tongue twister written on the chart paper, " Seals in the sea steal sneakers." Okay now let's say the tongue twister all together. Now we're going to stretch out the /E/ whenever you see the ea digraph. "Seeeeaaaals in the seeeeaaa steeeeeaaaal sneeeeeaaaakers." 3. "Okay I want to ask you guys some tricky questions and I want you to raise your hand if you know the answer and I will call on you! Each of you will get a chance to answer so if I do not call on you first do not get discouraged." "Do you hear the /E/ sound in each or 4. We will continue with a lesson similar to a letter box lesson, but it is adapted for whole group instruction. It will be played like "Hangman" in which each student will be able to guess a phoneme (this is the key difference; instead of representing graphemes, we will be representing phonemes). Write word blanks and set up the Hangman symbol on the chalk board. Model: "Who has ever played Hangman? (If any students have not, use this modeling experience to fill them in). We will be playing hangman with some words that have the digraph that we just learned. Here's how we play: Hmmm I see three blanks, so I know there will be three phonemes in the word. And the hint is Sea creatures (that's interesting, both of those words have ea=/E/!). So now I'm going to guess a letter t (continue with the hangman process until seal is spelled out [s ea l]). Continue with bean (3 phonemes blanks), treat (4 phoneme blanks), leap(3 phoneme blanks), sheath (3 phoneme blanks), beat (3 phoneme blanks), cream (4 phoneme blanks), and ear( 2 phoneme blanks). (Then have students read the list of words). 5. Next, we will go through the pictures of words containing the ea= /E/ digraph. Students will write the word, including the correct spelling on their individual dry erase boards. Pictures can include: leaf, peach, sea, peas, teapot, steam, seal, meal, speak, and sneakers. 7. Students will be divided into partners, preferably pairing strong and weaker readers together for partner reading. Partner 1 will read a page, while partner 2 stays engaged, asking questions, then vice versa. Before students begin, engage them with a short book talk: Dave and Bill's boat sins, and they are left adrift in the sea in a tiny rowboat. Let's find out if they can make it to Gull Rock 8. For the assessment, call students individually to your desk and have them read a list of pseudowords. Here is a list of ideas: freal, sheaz, deak, breals, veash. Title: How to Perform a Step Over Move in Soccer The step over move is a fundamental soccer skill that can help you get past defenders and create scoring opportunities. This move involves feinting to go one way with the ball before quickly shifting your body weight and moving in another direction. By mastering this technique, you'll be able to enhance your dribbling skills, improve your agility, and keep opponents guessing. Here are the steps to perform a successful step over move: ### 1. Master basic ball control Before attempting any advanced moves like the step over, ensure you have strong fundamentals in place. Practice controlling the ball with all parts of both feet, as well as receiving passes from different angles and heights. Being comfortable with the ball will allow you to focus more on executing the step-over instead of worrying about simply maintaining possession. ### 2. Choose the right moment Timing is crucial when performing a step over move. Identify an appropriate situation where the defender is applying pressure but has slightly overcommitted or left space open near their standing leg. Ideally, aim for moments when there is enough room for you to exploit once they commit to defending against your initial fake. Key tip: Be patient and wait for the perfect opportunity; rushing into a step over may result in losing possession or being caught off balance. ### 3. Set up the move As you approach the defender, position the ball slightly ahead of your dominant foot so that it's angled towards the side you plan to step over (left or right). Your non-dominant foot should point in the opposite direction, acting as a pivot point for the move. Keep your knees bent and lean your upper body slightly forward to maintain balance and generate power during the motion. ### 4. Execute the first touch Use the inside or outside part of your dominant foot to lightly tap the ball away from the defender while simultaneously lifting your other foot off the ground. The intention here is not to send the ball too far but rather to create just enough separation between yourself and the opponent. Key guideline: Focus on keeping the ball close to your body throughout the entire process to make it difficult for the defender to react or steal possession. ### Wiggle him in their oh I'm so glad you got it in their try another one. "Woe! woe!" wailed the Walk  re, speeding wildly away. oh, d' you want a napkin too. Note: Practice quizzes are available only for those sections of the manual covering rules of the road (Chapters 4 through 11 and Road Signs). Traffic signs tell you about traffic rules, special hazards, where you are, how to get where you are going and where services are available. The shape and color of traffic signs give clues to the type of information they provide: REGULATION SIGNS usually are white rectangles with black lettering or symbols, but some are different shapes, and some may use red letters or symbols. WARNING SIGNS usually are yellow and diamond-shaped, with black lettering or symbols. DESTINATION SIGNS are green with white letters and symbols. SERVICE SIGNS are blue with white letters and symbols. Know the signs shown below and what they mean. You will be asked about them on your written test. COLOR: Red, with white letters. MEANING: Come to a full stop, yield the right-of-way to vehicles and pedestrians in or approaching the intersection. Go when it is safe. You must come to a stop before the stop line, if there is one. If not, you must stop before entering the crosswalk. (See "Stop and Crosswalk Lines" under the "Pavement Markings" section of this chapter.) If there is no stop line or crosswalk, you must stop before entering the intersection, at the point nearest the intersection that gives you a view of traffic on the intersecting roadway. COLOR: Red and white, with red letters. MEANING: Slow down as you approach the intersection. Prepare to stop and yield the right-of-way to vehicles and pedestrians in or approaching the intersection. You must come to a full stop at a YIELD sign if traffic conditions require it. When you approach a YIELD sign, check carefully for traffic, and be prepared to stop. COLOR: White, with black and/or red letters or symbols. MEANING: These signs give information about rules for traffic direction, lane use, turning, speed, parking, and other special requirements. Some regulation signs have a red circle with a slash over a symbol indicating that an action, such as a right turn, is not allowed, or that certain vehicles are restricted from the road. Rectangular white signs with black or red letters or symbols are clues to be alert for special rules. COLOR: Yellow, with black letters or symbols. MEANING: You are approaching an especially hazardous location or a place where there is a special rule, as shown in the sample signs. Sometimes a warning sign is combined with a rectangular yellow and black "recommended speed" sign. This means reduced speed is advised in that area. COLOR: Yellow with black letters "RR" and "X" symbol. MEANING: There is a railroad crossing ahead. Use caution, and be prepared to stop. If you are following a bus or truck toward a railroad crossing, be careful. Most buses and some trucks must stop at railroad crossings. (See "Railroad Crossing Signals" later in this chapter.) COLOR: Orange, with black letters or symbols. MEANING: People are working on or near the roadway, and traffic may be controlled by a flag person. A work area speed limit as low as 25 MPH (40 km/h) may be posted. Even if no speed limit is posted, you must drive at a reduced speed through the work zone, and you must always obey flag persons. These illustrations show some of the signals a flag person will use. Know and obey them. COLOR: Green, with white lettering. MEANING: Show direction and distance to various locations. MEANING: Indicate interstate, U.S., state or county routes. The shape tells you what type of route you are on. The sample signs, left to right, are for state, U.S., and interstate routes. When planning a trip, use a highway map to decide which routes to take. During the trip, watch for destination signs so you will not get lost, or have to turn or stop suddenly. COLOR: Blue, with white letters or symbols. MEANING: Show the location of services, such as rest areas, gas stations, hospitals and campgrounds. Traffic lights are usually red, yellow and green from top to bottom, or left to right. At some intersections, there are single red, yellow or green lights. Some traffic lights are steady, others flash. Some are circular, and some are arrows. State law requires that if the traffic lights or controls are out of service or malfunctioning when you approach an intersection, you must come to a stop as you would for a stop sign. You must then proceed according to the rules of right of way, unless you are directed to proceed by a traffic officer. STEADY RED: Stop. Do not go until the light is green. If a green arrow is shown with the red light, you may go only in the direction of the arrow and only if the way is clear. You may make a right turn at a steady red light after coming to a full stop and yielding the right-of-way to oncoming traffic and pedestrians. You may make a left turn at a steady red light when turning from a one-way road into another one-way road after coming to a full stop and yielding the right-of-way to oncoming traffic and pedestrians. You may not make a turn at a red light if there is a NO TURN ON RED sign posted, or another sign, signal or pavement marking prohibits the turn. Also, turning on a red light is not allowed in New York City unless a sign is posted permitting it. The driver of a school bus carrying pupils may not turn right on any red light. FLASHING RED: Means the same as a STOP sign: Stop, yield the right-of-way, and go when it is safe. RED ARROW: Do not go in the direction of the arrow until the red arrow light goes out and a green light or arrow light goes on. A right or left turn on red is not permitted at a red arrow. STEADY YELLOW: The light is changing from green to red. Be ready to stop for the red light. FLASHING YELLOW: Drive with caution. YELLOW ARROW: The protection of a green arrow is ending. If you intend to turn in the direction of the arrow, be prepared to stop. STEADY GREEN: Go, but yield the right-of-way to other traffic at the intersection as required by law (see Chapter 5). GREEN ARROW: You may go in the direction of the arrow, but you must yield the right-of-way to other traffic at the intersection as required by law (see Chapter 5.) Special overhead lights are sometimes used to indicate which lanes of a highway may be used at certain times: STEADY RED "X": Do not drive in this lane. STEADY YELLOW "X": Move out of this lane. FLASHING YELLOW "X": This lane may only be used for a left turn. GREEN ARROW: You may use this lane. Flashing red lights, lowered crossing gates and/or a ringing bell at a railroad crossing mean that you must stop, at least 15 feet (5 m) from the tracks. Do not cross the tracks until the lights and bell have stopped and the crossing gates are all the way up. Do not drive around or under a gate that is being lowered or raised. Look and listen for trains before crossing any railroad tracks. If an approaching train is near enough or going fast enough to be a danger, you may not cross the tracks, even if there are no signals or the signals are not working. You may not cross any railroad tracks unless there is room for your vehicle on the other side. If other traffic prevents you from crossing all the way, wait, and cross only when there is room. School buses with or without passengers, other buses while carrying passengers, and vehicles carrying explosives or flammable cargo must stop at all railroad crossings. Keep this in mind if you are following one of these vehicles. Lines and symbols on the roadway divide lanes and tell you when you may pass other vehicles or change lanes, which lanes to use for turns, and where you must stop for signs or traffic signals. The arrows on these illustrations show the direction of traffic. Solid lines along the side of the road tell you where its edge is - where the travel lane ends and the road's shoulder begins. It is illegal to drive across the edge line, except when directed to do so by a police officer or other authorized official. An edge line which slants toward the center of the road shows that the road is narrower ahead. Lines separating lanes of traffic moving in the same direction are white. Lines separating traffic moving in opposite directions are yellow. There may be two lines between lanes, and lines may be solid or broken. Read Chapter 6 for the rules on passing other vehicles. Single broken line: You may pass other vehicles or change lanes if you can do so safely and not interfere with traffic. Solid line with broken line: If you're on the side with the solid line, you may not pass other vehicles or cross the line except to make a left turn into a driveway. If you're on the side with the broken line, you may pass if it is safe to do so and your driving will not interfere with traffic. Double solid lines: You may not pass, or change lanes. You may not cross the lines except when turning left to enter or leave the highway (e.g., to or from a driveway or to perform a U-turn see Chapter 5). Single solid line: You may pass other vehicles or change lanes, but you should do so only if obstructions in the road make it necessary or traffic conditions require it. Stop and Crosswalk Lines: At an intersection controlled by a STOP sign, YIELD sign or traffic light, there may be a white stop line painted across the lane, and/or two parallel lines painted across the road, forming a crosswalk. When required to stop because of a sign or light, you must stop before reaching the stop line, if there is one, or the crosswalk. You need only stop at a stop line or crosswalk if required to do so by a light, sign or traffic officer, but be careful to look out for pedestrians at any crosswalk. (See "Pedestrians" in Chapter 11). Arrows: Arrows show which lanes you must use. In this illustration, for example, you may turn right only from the right lane. If you are going straight, you must use the left lane. You should be in the proper lane before reaching the solid line which separates the lanes. Diamond Symbol: This symbol is used to designate reserved lanes for buses, HOV's (High Occupancy Vehicles) such as car-pools and van-pools, bicycles, or other special vehicles. You may not enter and use these lanes unless your vehicle complies with the occupancy or other requirements of the accompanying regulatory signs for the times the special conditions are in effect. When used to designate reserved lanes on city streets, sections of the solid white line separating the diamond lanes from the regular lanes may be replaced by dashed white lines. In these locations, non-HOV's may enter the HOV lane if they make a right turn at the next intersection. Bus lanes and HOV lanes are used to promote the most efficient use of limited street and highway capacity by assuring that vehicles with the highest priority move the fastest. Directions given by traffic officers take precedence over signs, signals or pavement markings. If a traffic officer signals you to stop at a green light, for example, you must stop. If an officer signals you to drive through a red light or stop sign, you must do so. Among those authorized to direct traffic are police officers, peace officers such as on-duty auxiliary or fire police, highway work area flag persons, and school crossing guards. [Row announces she will leave to see if it helps with the activity] Title: How to Delete Your Stardoll Account Stardoll is a popular online fashion game where players can create their own doll, design clothes, and socialize with other users. However, if you've decided that it's time to move on from Stardoll, this tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting your account permanently. Please note that this action cannot be undone, so make sure you have made up your mind before proceeding. **Step 1:** Log into your Stardoll account To start the account deletion process, first log in using your username or email address and password at <https://www.stardoll.com/>. This step ensures that you are able to access your account settings and perform necessary actions. **Step 2:** Access My Account page Once logged in, hover over the 'Me' button located at the top right corner of the screen. A drop-down menu should appear; click on 'My Account.' The My Account page contains all essential information about your membership status, billing details, security options, and more importantly, the option to delete your account. **Step 3:** Go to the Security tab On the left side of the My Account page, find and click on the 'Security' tab. It houses various privacy and safety features related to your account. Here, you'll find the link to initiate the deletion request. **Step 4:** Initiate account closure Scroll down until you see the section labeled as 'Close my account'. Click on the text link below it which reads 'here', taking you to another page detailing what happens when an account gets deleted. Read through these points carefully before continuing. When ready, click on the red button saying 'Yes, close my account now.' Key tip: Make sure to save any important data (like designs or items) outside of Stardoll prior to closing your account since they won't be recoverable after deletion. **Step 5:** Confirm Email Address You will be asked to confirm your registered email address associated with your Stardoll account. Enter your email correctly then hit the blue 'Send confirmation code' button. Check your inbox for an email containing a verification code. Copy the exact code sent to you. **Step 6:** Verify email address and enter reason Return to the Stardoll window where you requested the verification code. Paste the copied code into the box provided and press 'Verify'. After successful validation, you'll need to provide a reason why you want to delete your account from the given list of options. Select one that best fits your situation. **Step 7:** Final Confirmation Review your decision one last time by clicking on the red 'Confirm & Close Account' button. Congratulations! You have successfully initiated the deletion process of your Stardoll account. Additional Guidelines: * Deleting your account does not cancel any active SuperStar or Elite Club subscriptions. Be sure to manage those separately via iTunes, Google Play Store, or PayPal depending on how you paid. * According to Stardoll's policy, accounts closed due to violation of terms may not be eligible for permanent removal. * Expect to receive a final email once your account has been officially deleted. If it doesn't arrive within two weeks, consider contacting Stardoll support for assistance. [looks through bag and finds the rubber band.] Why do you have a bad cough? Um and the other labs as well. Title: Using Lists to Solve Math Problems Have you ever played with lists to organize things? Maybe you've made a shopping list or a to-do list. In math, we also use lists to help us solve problems! Let me show you an example using something called "variables" and "constraints." Don't worry - it's easier than it sounds! Imagine you have six tasks (we'll call them Task 1, Task 2, , Task 6) and each task has a certain amount of points associated with it (let's say 10, 20, 10, 30, 20, and 10). Your goal is to earn exactly 100 points by choosing exactly one number from each task's point options. You write down your choices in the order of the tasks: Task 1: ___, Task 2: ___, ... , Task 6: ___ For example, one possible way to earn 100 points could be: Task 1: 10, Task 2: 20, Task 3: 10, Task 4: 30, Task 5: 20, Task 6: 10 Now, let's see how we can create a list for each task and use those lists to tell our "math problem solver" which numbers to pick so that everything adds up to 100 points. We're going to use Gurobi, a tool that helps find solutions to complex problems like ours! First, we need to define our tasks' point options as lists: task\_points = [[10, 20, 30], [20, 30], [10], [10, 30], [10, 20, 30, 40], [10, 20, 30]] Next, we add decision variables z1, z2, ..., z6 representing our choices for each task: my\_model = Model() f = [1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1] # same weight for all tasks z = my\_model.addVars(6, lb=1, ub=100, vtype=GRB.INTEGER) # integers between 1 and 100 We then specify that the product of the chosen value for each variable (zi * fi) should sum to 100: my\_model.addConstr(z.prod(f) == 100, name="") Finally, we ask Gurobi to maximize the total points earned (which will actually give us the desired result since multiplying any positive number by itself always results in a greater value): my\_model.setObjective(z.prod(f), sense=GRB.MAXIMIZE) When we run this model, Gurobi finds a solution where the selected values come from their respective lists. Now, don't you feel smart having solved this complex sounding problem?! By breaking it into smaller parts and organizing information using lists, even young learners can tackle seemingly difficult challenges. Happy exploring! So he was all dirty when he went to school. [View the accompanying Practice Problems on Molar Mass Conversions here.] What is Molar Mass? Molar mass is literally the mass of one mole of a substance. We saw in a previous lesson on atomic mass that the periodic table provides us with the average atomic mass of elements. For example, the atomic mass of hydrogen (symbol H) is 1.01 amu (atomic mass units). But one incredibly handy aspect of the periodic table is that those same masses are also the mass of one mole of the element in grams. For example, one atom of hydrogen has a mass of 1.01 amu, but one mole of hydrogen has a mass of 1.01 g. Remember that one mole is equal to Avogardo's number, 6.022 1023 particles. So one mole of hydrogen, or 6.022 1023 hydrogen atoms, has a mass of 1.01 g. |Element||Atomic Mass||Molar Mass||Number of atoms| |H||1.01 amu||1.01 g/mol||6.022 1023| |Mg||24.31 amu||24.31 g/mol||6.022 1023| |Na||22.99 amu||22.99 g/mol||6.022 1023| Molar Mass of Compounds To find the mass of one mole of any substance we count the elements present and add up their atomic masses. For example, let's calculate the molar mass of iron(III) oxide, Fe2O3. Fe2O3 is composed of two iron atoms and three oxygen atoms. To calculate its molar mass we have to add up the mass of two iron atoms and three oxygen atoms. 2 x Fe = 2 x 55.85 = 111.70 3 x O = 3 x 16.00 = + 48.00 Total = 159.70 g/mol This is the molar mass of Fe2O3. So one mole of Fe2O3 would have a mass of 159.70 g. Or Avogadro's number, 6.022 1023, of Fe2O3 formula units (ionic compound particles) would have a mass of 159.70 g. [In terms of significant digits, we normally just keep as many decimal places for the total mass as was originally present in the mass of the element. For example, iron (Fe) has a mass of 55.85 - two decimal places. So when we add up two iron atoms, we keep two decimal places and write 111.70.] Calculate the molar mass of: There are 5 Mn atoms (mass of 54.94) and 16 O atoms (mass of 16.00). 5 x 54.94 + 16 x 16.00 = 530.70 g/mol There are 2 Al atoms (mass of 26.98), 3 S atoms (mass of 32.07), and 12 O atoms (mass of 16.00). 2 x 26.98 + 3 x 32.07 + 12 x 16.00 = 342.17 g/mol Notice that the units on molar mass are grams per mole (g/mol). This looks a lot like a conversion factor that relates two units: grams to moles. Just like there are 12 inches/1 foot, for Al2(SO4)3 there are 342.17 g/mol. This allows us to convert between grams of Al2(SO4)3 and moles of Al2(SO4)3. The following two example problems will illustrate how to use molar mass as a conversion factor between moles and mass. 3. What is the mass in grams of 3.81 moles of Fe2O3? We earlier calculated the molar mass of Fe2O3 to be 159.70 g/mol. mol Fe2O3 x 159.70 g/1 mol Fe2O3 = 608 g 4. How many moles are in 713 grams of Al2(SO4)3? The molar mass of Al2(SO4)3 was earlier calculated to be 342.17 g/mol. Since we are converting grams into moles, we need to flip this conversion factor so that grams are on the bottom in order to cancel. g x 1 mol Al2(SO4)3/342.17 g = 2.08 mol Al2(SO4)3 [Get more Practice Problems on Molar Mass Conversions here.] So we did that for a long time. Using non-standard units of measure is a great way to introduce children to the concept of measuring. Legos, blocks, and even dinosaur feet make great measuring sticks for a young child. Once they have an understanding of the basic concept of measurement and have had plenty of opportunities to explore, then we can introduce them to standard units of measure (inches, feet, etc). Here's what you'll need: a dinosaur footprint-if a dinosaur doesn't make a stop at your house anytime soon, you can make one on a large posterboard. :) We also used paint to make an imprint of Big Brother's footprint as well. This made it easier to compare the dinosaur foot to his foot. For 2-year olds: - Begin by letting your child walk on the footprint and compare her foot to the dinosaur's. - Ask her which is bigger and which is smaller - Count the toes on one of her feet and then count the dinosaur's toes. Ask which is more 5 or 3? If she isn't sure, take out 3 blocks and then 5 blocks and line them up. Then ask which is more. For 3-years and up: - Do all of the activities for 2-year olds above - Ask your how many of her feet equals a dinosaur foot. You might have to explain what the term equals means. - Make a table on a piece of construction paper. Draw a few items on the left-hand side of the table that your child would like to measure. Make two columns at the top of the table-one for the dinosaur foot and one for your child's foot. Measure the items and then compare. It provides a comprehensive overview of the steps required to become a successful stripper, including physical preparation, mental preparation, and practical aspects such as finding employment and building a client base. Please note that stripping can be a physically and emotionally demanding job, and may not be suitable for everyone. Always ensure that you are comfortable with your decision before proceeding. Step 1: Assess Your Suitability Before embarking on a career as a stripper, it's important to assess whether this line of work is right for you. Consider the following factors: * Are you comfortable with nudity and being objectified? * Can you handle rejection and criticism? * Are you able to separate your personal life from your professional life? * Do you have the necessary physical attributes (such as a fit body and attractive features)? * Are you willing to put in the time and effort required to succeed in this competitive industry? Getting loads of GP (in-game currency) with rune essence in RuneScape is an effective money-making method that requires some time investment but can yield significant rewards. This guide will walk you through the process in detail, providing clear instructions and helpful tips along the way. **Step 1: Acquire Rune Essence** To start, you need to have access to Rune Essence. There are two ways to obtain this resource: mining or purchasing from other players. Mining rune essence involves traveling to various locations like the Rune Essence Mine northwest of Varrock or the quarry south of Falador, then using a pickaxe to extract the raw material from the ground. Alternatively, you may buy rune essence from the Grand Exchange or directly from other players at a slightly higher price. *Key Tip:* Consider investing in a high-level pickaxe (such as a Dragon or Crystal Pickaxe) if you plan to mine your own rune essence. These tools increase your mining speed, reducing overall gathering time. **Step 2: Teleportation Method** Next, decide which teleportation method works best for you. The most common options include using a combination of a Games Necklace, Amulet of Glory, or Ring of Dueling, alongside the appropriate home teleport spell. Utilizing these items allows you to quickly travel between your bank and the rune essence location, minimizing downtime. *Key Guideline:* Keep track of your inventory space when transporting large quantities of rune essence; running out of room mid-transport can result in wasted time returning to the bank. **Step 3: Crafting Runes** Now that you've acquired your rune essence, head over to one of the many altars scattered throughout Gielinor where you can craft runes by using the essence on the altar. Each type of rune yields different amounts of GP per essence, so choose wisely based on current market prices and demand. Common choices among players include Air, Water, Earth, Fire, Mind, Chaos, and Death runes due to their consistent profitability. *Key Tip:* Regularly check the Grand Exchange for updated pricing information on all types of runes to ensure you're making the most profitable decisions possible. **Step 4: Selling Your Runes** Once you've crafted your chosen runes, sell them either via the Grand Exchange or directly to other players. When selling on the exchange, pay close attention to the 'buy limit,' ensuring you don't exceed it to avoid being flagged for suspicious activity. Direct player sales typically occur in popular trading areas such as World 301 in Edgeville or inside the Grand Exchange itself. *Key Guideline:* Always be cautious when engaging in direct player trades, taking care to verify the legitimacy of potential buyers before proceeding. **Step 5: Repeat Process** Lastly, rinse and repeat Steps 1-4 until satisfied with your accumulated GP. As you progress through the game, consider upgrading equipment and utilizing more efficient methods, further increasing your profits over time. *Key Tip:* To maximize efficiency during repetitive tasks like this, consider setting personal goals or challenges to keep yourself motivated and engaged. For example, aim to earn a specific amount of GP within a set period or strive to improve your crafting speed consistently. Once upon a time, there was a little girl. Title: How to Get a Patronus on Pottermore Pottermore is an online platform created by J.K. Rowling that allows fans of the Harry Potter series to delve deeper into the magical world she has created. One of the most exciting features of Pottermore is the ability to discover your own Patronus, which is a powerful protector charm that takes the form of an animal. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to get a Patronus on Pottermore: **Step 1: Create a Pottermore Account** If you haven't already done so, sign up for a free account at www.pottermore.com. You will need to provide some basic information such as your name, birthdate, and email address. Once registered, log in using your new credentials. **Step 2: Navigate to the Patronus Test** After logging in, navigate to the main menu located at the top left corner of the screen (three horizontal lines). From there, click on "Magical Creatures" then select "Patronus." This should bring you to the introduction page about Patronuses. Click on the button labeled "Take the test" when ready. **Step 3: Begin the Test** The Patronus test consists of several questions designed to gauge various aspects of your personality. Each question presents two statements; choose the one that resonates more with you. There are no right or wrong answers here - just go with what feels truest to who you are. The goal of these questions is to match your unique character traits to a specific animal patronus. **Key Tips:** * Be honest: Since this process matches your responses to potential Patronus animals, answer honestly instead of trying to second-guess what might give you a particular result. * Take your time: Don't rush through the questions. Consider each statement carefully before making your choice. * Stay consistent: Try to maintain consistency throughout the quiz. Shifting from one extreme to another between questions may affect the accuracy of your results. **Step 4: Review Your Results** At the end of the test, you will be presented with your Patronus animal along with its description. Read through the details to understand why this animal was chosen based on your answers. Feel free to explore other possible Patronus meanings if they interest you. **Step 5: Practice Casting Your Patronus Charm** Now comes the fun part! After learning your Patronus type, head over to the 'Spells' section under 'Learn Spells & Charms'. Locate the 'Expecto Patronum' spell and practice casting it within the designated area. Keep practicing until you can successfully cast it without any errors. **Key Guidelines:** * Focus on happy memories: Remember, conjuring a Patronus requires recalling a particularly joyful memory. Make sure to pick something vivid and uplifting. * Use correct pronunciation: Correctly pronounce 'Expecto Patronum' while visualizing your chosen memory. * Maintain concentration: Concentrate hard during the entire incantation process. Any distraction could disrupt the formation of your Patronus. By following these steps meticulously, you'll soon find out your Patronus on Pottermore, providing yet another way to connect with the enchanting universe crafted by J.K. Rowling. If you're reading this, chances are that you too have been bitten by the 'Potter bug.' But being a true fan involves more than just watching the movies or reading the books - it requires an understanding and appreciation of every minute detail in the sprawling universe created by Rowling over two decades ago. This comprehensive guide will provide all the necessary steps towards becoming a devoted Harry Potter aficionado. Step 1: Read All Seven Books Key Tip: Pay Attention to Every Detail Reading the seven-book series is essential if you want to call yourself a true Harry Potter fan. Each book offers unique insights into the characters, plots, and magical elements within the story. As you read, take note of character development arcs, intricate plot twists, and hidden details woven throughout the narrative. These nuances enrich your overall experience and deepen your connection with the storyline. Remember, there's always something new to discover upon re-reading! Step 2: Watch All Eight Movies (And Maybe Rewatch Them) Key Guideline: Observe Carefully During Viewings While nothing can replace the richness provided by the books, the films also contain numerous gems worth appreciating. Pay close attention during viewings; notice how certain scenes were adapted from page to screen, observe the costumes, settings, and special effects, and listen closely to dialogues. [removes man and washer from the black base] Title: How to Convert Fahrenheit to Celsius Degrees Converting temperatures from Fahrenheit to Celsius is a fundamental skill often required in various fields such as meteorology, physics, chemistry, engineering, and even everyday cooking activities. This tutorial will provide you with an easy-to-understand, step-by-step guide on converting Fahrenheit degrees to their equivalent Celsius values. By following these instructions diligently, you'll be able to perform accurate conversions swiftly and confidently. ### Step 1: Understanding the Temperature Scales Before diving into the conversion process, let's familiarize ourselves with both temperature scales involved: * **Fahrenheit ( F)**: Developed by Gabriel Daniel Fahrenheit in 1724, this scale sets the freezing point of water at 32 F and its boiling point at 212 F under standard atmospheric conditions. * **Celsius ( C)**: Also known as centigrade, this scale was proposed by Anders Celsius in 1742. It defines the freezing point of water as 0 C and the boiling point as 100 C under standard atmospheric conditions. The relationship between these two scales can be mathematically expressed as follows: `T( C) = [T( F) - 32] * 5/9` This formula allows us to convert any given temperature value in Fahrenheit to its corresponding Celsius degree value. Let's break down this equation further to understand each component better. ### Step 2: Subtracting 32 from the Given Fahrenheit Value The first operation in our conversion process involves subtracting 32 from the provided Fahrenheit temperature value: `T( C) = [T( F) - 32]` By doing so, we are shifting the Fahrenheit scale's origin (freezing point of water) to align with that of the Celsius scale (also the freezing point of water). For example, if we have a temperature of 86 F, after subtracting 32, we get 54. ### Step 3: Multiplying the Result by 5/9 Once we have obtained the shifted Fahrenheit value, we then multiply it by a factor of `5/9`. This scaling operation ensures proper alignment of the boiling points of water in both scales: `T( C) = [T( F) - 32] * 5/9` Applying this multiplication to our previous example (54), results in approximately 12.22 C. Thus, a temperature of 86 F is equivalent to around 12.22 C. #### Key Tips & Guidelines * Always double-check your calculations for accuracy. * Keep track of significant figures when performing arithmetic operations during conversions. * Remember that negative temperatures also require conversion using the same formula. Simply plug in the negative Fahrenheit value and proceed with the calculation steps accordingly. * Excel or Google Sheets users may utilize built-in functions like `CONVERT()` or manual formulas to simplify repetitive tasks. In conclusion, mastering the art of converting Fahrenheit to Celsius entails understanding the underlying principles behind both temperature scales and applying mathematical transformations accurately. With practice and patience, anyone can readily adapt to switching between different temperature measurement units. Have you ever tried to solve a puzzle where you need to figure out what goes in a box based on some clues or rules? Solving equations can sometimes feel like working on a puzzle. Today, let's explore a special kind of equation involving functions and their inverses. Imagine that we have a magical bag transformation machine, called "f". You can put things into the machine, and it will give you something new based on its secret rule. We call these inputs "x", and the outputs "f(x)". The collection of all possible inputs is named "A", while the collection of all possible outputs is named "B". So our magical machine transforms elements from set A into elements from set B. Now, imagine there's another magic device which undoes the work of the first machine! That's the inverse function, written as f^-1. When you feed the output of the first machine back into the second one, it gives you your original input back. In other words, putting "f(x)" through the inverse machine results in getting "x" again. Our goal is to understand why applying the function and then its inverse, in any order, doesn't change the final result. Mathematically speaking, we want to show that f(f^-1(f(X))) equals f(X). To make sense of this, let's think about our magical machines once more. Imagine you put a group of items (set X) from set A into the first machine. It changes them into a new bunch of stuff - f(X). Now, take those new items and pass them through the inverse machine; you get back to your original group, but now we call it f^-1(f(X)). Finally, apply the initial machine again by taking each item inside f^-1(f(X)), and running them through the first machine. Since every item has been transformed twice - once going in and once coming out - everything returns exactly the same as before, thus proving that f(f^-1(f(X))) indeed equals f(X). So here's the big reveal: no matter how many times you alternate between using a function and its inverse, the end result remains unchanged! Just like when playing with interconnected puzzle pieces, different routes may lead us astray momentarily, but ultimately, we always return to the starting point. [removes the pop beads from the container] Citing a Website: A Comprehensive Guide ====================================== Introduction ------------ When writing papers or reports, you may need to cite websites as sources of information. Properly citing your sources acknowledges the work of other authors and allows your reader to locate the original material if needed. This guide will walk you through the process of creating citations for websites using the American Psychological Association (APA) format, which is commonly used in social sciences. However, these principles can be applied to various citation styles such as Modern Language Association (MLA), Chicago Manual of Style (Chicago), and others with minor modifications. ### Key Tips and Guidelines --------------------------- * Always provide complete and accurate information about your source. * Maintain consistency in formatting across all citations. * Double-check any automated tools that generate citations; they might not always be correct. * Familiarize yourself with the specific requirements of your institution or publisher. General Format -------------- An APA website citation includes the following elements: 1. Author(s). 2. Year of publication (in parentheses). 3. Title of the webpage/article. 4. URL (without 'http' or 'https'). 5. Date accessed (if applicable). Here's an example: ```bibtex AuthorLastName, A. A. (Year). Title of the article or webpage. Site Name/Publisher. Sarah knew that he was being kind and trying to protect her from being too rough with the puppy. But a rude man started shouting at them. "Don't touch my dog!" he said. It scared both of them, so David took Sarah's hand and they ran away. As soon as they were far enough away, David gave Sarah a big hug. "That was a bit scary, wasn't it?" David said. Sarah nodded her head, glad to have her friend with her. David and Sarah both knew that they had each other when something scary happened. With a big smile, they started to walk home together. Creating a Facebook profile is an easy and straightforward process that can be completed in just a few simple steps. With over 2.8 billion active users, Facebook is one of the most popular social networking platforms in the world, making it a great place to connect with friends, family, and colleagues. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to create a Facebook profile, including detailed explanations, key tips, and best practices to ensure a positive experience. Step 1: Open your web browser and navigate to www.facebook.com. This step takes you directly to the Facebook homepage where you can begin setting up your new account. By using a reputable web browser such as Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, or Microsoft Edge, you'll have access to enhanced security features, faster loading times, and improved user interfaces. Step 2: Click on the "Create New Account" button located at the top right corner of the page. By clicking this button, you are indicating that you wish to sign up for a new Facebook account. As creating multiple accounts violates Facebook's terms of service, starting with a fresh profile ensures that you adhere to their policies and maintain a single, authentic online presence. Step 3: Fill out the registration form with accurate information. The registration form requires basic personal details like your name, email address (or phone number), password, date of birth, and gender. Providing accurate information allows Facebook to verify your identity and deliver relevant content tailored to your interests. Keep in mind that some fields may be optional depending on your region. * Key Tip: Choose a strong password containing uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and symbols to protect your privacy and prevent unauthorized access to your account. Avoid sharing your login credentials with others and consider enabling two-factor authentication for added security. Step 4: Read and accept Facebook's Statement of Rights and Responsibilities and Privacy Policy. These documents outline Facebook's rules governing user behavior and data handling practices. Familiarizing yourself with these policies enables you to understand your rights as a user, abide by community standards, and make informed decisions about what information you share on the platform. Step 5: Verify your email address or mobile phone number. Facebook sends a verification code to either your email address or mobile phone number to confirm your identity. Enter the received code into the designated field within the allotted time frame to proceed with the setup process. Verifying your contact information enhances your account's security and prevents fraudulent activities. Step 6: Customize your profile settings and preferences. After verifying your contact information, you are prompted to add a profile picture and cover photo, search for friends, and input additional details about yourself (e.g., education, work history, relationship status). Configuring these options allows you to establish a unique online persona and engage more effectively with other users. * Guideline: Select appropriate photos and limit the amount of personally identifiable information shared publicly. Maintaining discretion when disclosing sensitive data reduces potential risks associated with oversharing and safeguards your digital footprint. Step 7: Begin exploring Facebook's features and connecting with people. Now that your profile is set up, start engaging with content posted by friends, join groups related to your interests, and participate in discussions on public pages. Building connections and interacting regularly strengthens your online network and fosters meaningful relationships with others. In conclusion, following this thorough tutorial provides you with a solid foundation for establishing a successful Facebook profile. By understanding each step and applying the recommended tips and guidelines, you can enjoy a safe, enjoyable, and fulfilling social media experience while maintaining control over your online presence. A lower case B (b) is used to indicate a flat sign in text documents. There is a half step between E-F and B-C naturally, so that Fb is the same note as E, and Cb is the same as B. For now think of these notes by there natural names only (E and B). A flat remains in effect for the entire measure, or until it is canceled by a natural sign (). Once you cross into the next measure the flat is canceled out. Flats, sharps and natural signs that temporarily change the pitch of a note are known as accidentals. * Note that a courtesy accidental in parenthesis is often used in the next measure after a flat of the same letter name is used. This is not necessary but is often done to avoid confusion. Q. Hey cyberfret dude... how do I go down 1 fret from an open string (e.g. E down to Eb)? A. First you have to have a basic understanding of how the guitar is tuned (see basic tuning) You can't go down 1 fret from and open string, but you can go down 1 fret from the same note played on a different string. For example when you tune the 1st string on the guitar, you will play the 5th fret on the 2nd string. The first string open and the 5th fret on the second string are both E. I you want to play and E flat, just move down 1 fret from the E on the 2nd string. In the following exercise play the first 2 notes (A and Ab) with you 4th finger. For all other notes use 1 finger per fret (1st finger=1st fret, 2nd finger=2nd fret etc...) Be sure to name the notes to yourself as you play. I didn't even know we are in... Title: How to Change Your Homepage on Google Chrome Google Chrome is one of the most popular web browsers available today, known for its speed, efficiency, and customizable features. One such feature is the ability to set your preferred homepage, which is the web page that appears every time you open a new tab or window in Chrome. This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing your homepage on Google Chrome, providing in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines along the way. **Step 1:** Open Google Chrome Locate the Google Chrome icon on your computer's desktop, taskbar, or application folder and double-click it to launch the browser. If you cannot find the icon, search for 'Google Chrome' using your system's search function (e.g., Spotlight on macOS or Start Menu on Windows). **Step 2:** Access Google Chrome settings Click on the three vertical dots located at the top right corner of the Chrome window. A dropdown menu will appear; click on 'Settings' near the bottom of this list. Alternatively, type chrome://settings directly into the address bar and press Enter to access the Settings page immediately. **Step 3:** Navigate to the Appearance section Scroll down until you see the 'Appearance' section within the left sidebar. Clicking on it will reveal several options related to how Chrome looks and feels when you use it. **Step 4:** Set your homepage Under the 'Appearance' heading, locate the subheading titled 'Show home button.' Toggle the switch next to it so that it turns blue, indicating the option has been enabled. Once activated, two additional boxes will become visible beneath it: 'New Tab Page' and 'Enter custom web address.' To change your homepage, select the latter box, then enter the URL of your chosen website without any spaces or special characters (for example, https://www.wikipedia.org). Make sure to include 'http://' or 'https://' before the domain name. By setting your homepage to a specific web address, you ensure quicker access to frequently visited sites and create a consistent starting point whenever you open a new tab or window in Chrome. Additionally, having a dedicated homepage can help reduce distractions by minimizing the number of tabs needed during regular browsing sessions. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * **Use an Incognito Window**: Changing your homepage while logged into your Google account may save these preferences across devices linked to the same account. Use an incognito window if you want to modify your homepage settings only for the current device. * **Bookmark Important Pages**: Bookmark pages you visit often as shortcuts on your bookmarks bar instead of relying solely on your homepage. This allows you to maintain easy access to multiple websites from anywhere in Chrome without being limited to just one default page. * **Check Browser Extensions**: Some extensions might alter your homepage settings unexpectedly. Disable or remove any suspicious extensions if issues persist after following this tutorial. * **Keep Software Updated**: Regularly update your operating system and web browser to benefit from security patches, bug fixes, and performance improvements. These updates also contain newly added features that could enhance your overall browsing experience. Title: How to Crack a Master Combination Padlock Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of cracking a standard three-number combination lock, specifically focusing on Master Lock brand padlocks. This method is non-destructive and relies on understanding the mechanics and design of the lock itself. Please note that this information should be used responsibly and ethically; never attempt to break into someone else's property without proper authorization. ### Things You'll Need: * A Master Lock combination padlock (typically 40-50mm in size) * Patience and determination Step 1: Understanding the Mechanics A typical combination lock has several components-a shackle, a U-shaped bar, a drive tumbler, and three numbered wheels or spindles called flywheels with tiny internal teeth. When the correct sequence of numbers is entered, these teeth align perfectly, enabling the lock to open. The goal is to find this sequence by manipulating the lock and listening closely to its response. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be patient as the entire process may take up to an hour or more depending on your experience level. * Maintain focus and avoid distractions during the process. * Develop a consistent rhythm when turning the dial and applying pressure. * Listen carefully for subtle changes in sound and feel while rotating the dial. Step 2: Orientation and Preparation Hold the lock so that the shackle points upward at about a 30-degree angle from horizontal. With your other hand, grip the body of the lock just below the dials between thumb and index finger. Place your middle finger over the top of the dial to provide downward force while turning. Step 3: First Number - Finding the Racking Point Gently but firmly pull outwards on the shackle using your free hand while simultaneously pushing down on the dial with your middle finger. Turn the dial counterclockwise until resistance stops further rotation. At some point before reaching maximum resistance, there will be a slight decrease in tension followed immediately by another increase - this is known as the "racking point." Stop exactly at this racking point. This first number usually falls within the range of 0-36. Step 4: Second Number - Setting the Gate Position Without releasing any pressure applied in Step 3, turn the dial clockwise past zero until it cannot move anymore. Then continue slowly rotating the dial counterclockwise back towards zero until it catches again (you might hear or feel a click). Once caught, release all pressure on the shackle and allow the dial to spin freely. Record this second number since it represents the position where one set of flywheel teeth have aligned with the gate opening inside the U-bar assembly. Step 5: Third Number - Aligning Flywheel Teeth Now we need to locate the exact alignment required for unlocking the padlock. To do this, reapply gentle downward force onto the dial as done previously. Keeping constant contact, start turning the dial counterclockwise very slowly. As you approach the third number, you will notice increased friction which indicates the final tooth engagement happening. Upon feeling this increased drag, stop immediately and record this last digit. Step 6: Opening the Lock Maintaining steady downward pressure, gently tug the shackle straight outward. If everything went according to plan, the lock should now open! Conclusion: Remember that practice makes perfect; don't get discouraged if initial attempts aren't successful. It takes time and patience to master this technique. Always consider ethical implications whenever attempting to crack a lock without permission. You want to know why he gave this to you? I think I'll try it this way. Now they don't quite match, do they? Four boys born within the last two months that we know. Do you want to take your pictures home MEC? Where the grass is short and then you can walk around easier. The human should not be able to realise it is interacting with a computer. Yep, all the time I drink all the water. And as soon as they get out what happens? That night when the moon rose Hansel took Gretel by her hand and followed the tiny moonlit stones. How NOT To Write: passive constructions Why are they called "constructions"? Because a sentence is something you build, right? You assemble it with words. And those words can be active or passive. But they should be active. What's the difference? This: PASSIVE: Mistakes were made by me. ACTIVE: I made mistakes. PASSIVE: A good time was had by all. ACTIVE: We all had a good time. So. Active sentences are SHORTER and use fewer syllables. But that's not all they have going for them. They are DIRECT. They are simple. They are easy to follow. SUBJECT - VERB - OBJECT. Easy as pie. EXAMPLE: "I ran home." In an active construction, you literally start at the beginning with the subject: I. Then you take a step into the motion: ran. Then you end at the place where the subject ends: home. Literally, you move through the sentence in the same way that the person running does. You move with them, both physically across the page and mentally with the words. And that sympathy between your eyes and your mind helps close the gap between just reading about something and feeling like you're there. Now try this: "Home was run to by me." How wonky is that? You've got the beginning at the end, and the end at the beginning, and all sorts of unnecessary words in between. You've got your object where your subject should be, and you've got your subject as part of a prepositional phrase! OY! Stay simple unless you have a reason to get crazy. There are a gazillion ways to riff on the standard S-V-O construction, and plenty of cool things to do with form. I'm all for a syncopated rhythm in writing. But not with passive voice. Be strong! Be active! Be totally brazen! But don't insult your readers by asking them to do acrobatics across the page. This move allows you to change direction quickly, maintain speed, and carve gracefully along curved surfaces like pools or ramps. By mastering this technique, you will enhance your overall balance, agility, and style as a skater. Follow these steps carefully to learn how to do a cutback on a skateboard. ### Things You'll Need: * A standard skateboard with grip tape * Appropriate safety gear (helmet, knee pads, elbow pads, and wrist guards) * Smooth, flat surface or skate park with transition features such as quarter pipes, bowls, or banks **Step 1: Familiarize Yourself With Basic Stance and Equipment** Before attempting a cutback, ensure that you are comfortable standing on your skateboard and have developed basic skills like pushing off, balancing, turning, and stopping. Make sure your skateboard has good quality grip tape for traction and choose a location without obstacles or uneven terrain. Always wear appropriate protective gear when practicing new tricks. **Step 2: Gain Speed and Approach the Transition** Find a suitable transitional feature at the skatepark, such as a banked curve or quarter pipe. Begin by gaining momentum on a straightaway before approaching the transition. Bend your knees slightly and lean forward, preparing yourself for the upcoming turn. Keep both feet firmly planted over the trucks, maintaining equal weight distribution between them. ![Skateboard Tricks - Carving](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Carve-on-a-Skateboard-Step-3-Version-4.jpg/aid678950-v4- "P. vivax" and "P. ovale" can live in the liver for a long time. Ebola virus or Ebola virus disease (EVD), often shortened to Ebola is a very dangerous virus. It belongs to the family Filoviridae and is responsible for a severe disease characterized by the sudden onset of hemorrhagic fever accompanied by other non specific signs and symptoms. "Hemorrhagic" means that the victim will bleed a lot, inside and outside their body. The virus attacks almost every organ and tissue of the human body resulting in multi-organ dysfunction. Out of every 100 people who get Ebola, on average 25 to 90 die. There are four kinds of Ebola virus that can cause the disease. The virus was first found in Sudan.
